topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop a lightweight, low-latency adaptive quantum error correction framework optimized for resource-constrained mobile NISQ quantum edge devices that dynamically adjusts error-correcting codes and syndrome extraction strategies based on real-time device telemetry and intermittent network connectivity.

BACKGROUND: Mobile quantum edge devices are severely limited by power, size, and thermal constraints that preclude the use of existing QEC techniques designed for stationary or cloud-connected quantum systems. Current state-of-the-art QEC approaches, such as surface codes or low-overhead codes like those in “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices” (2026), require too much computational and qubit overhead to be practical in mobile environments. The open problem is enabling robust logical qubit fidelity maintenance with minimal computational resources while tolerating intermittent connectivity and dynamic environmental noise.

APPROACH: This work proposes a lightweight adaptive QEC algorithm that leverages real-time qubit error syndrome data combined with device telemetry (power consumption, temperature, and noise spectral densities) to dynamically select from a family of low-overhead QEC codes (e.g., tailored small-distance surface codes, Bacon-Shor codes) and adapt syndrome measurement frequency. The method incorporates an intermittent connectivity-aware feedback mechanism that enables local error correction when disconnected and synchronizes correction metadata opportunistically to a cloud or edge server. A novel lightweight syndrome decoding based on classical compressed sensing techniques reduces computational cost compared to standard decoding algorithms.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on IBM Quantum Experience datasets (e.g., IBM Quantum QASM simulator noise models), Google Quantum AI’s Sycamore NISQ device calibration datasets, and Rigetti’s Aspen-9 noise profiles to simulate realistic mobile edge noise environments. Baselines include the Low-Overhead QEC Codes for NISQ Devices (2026), Lightweight Adaptive QEC for Mobile Quantum Edge Devices (tournament winner), and standard Surface Code decoding methods. Metrics: achieve >30% reduction in logical qubit error rate compared to Low-Overhead QEC baseline, reduce syndrome decoding latency by at least 40%, and lower power consumption of error correction by >25%. Conduct ablation studies across adaptive code selection, syndrome measurement frequency, and connectivity-aware synchronization. Experiments run on simulated mobile NISQ hardware with 5 random seeds each, totaling 15 runs per configuration.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior stationary or cloud-dependent QEC methods, this approach explicitly integrates adaptive code selection and syndrome measurement frequency tailored to the unique constraints of mobile quantum edge hardware and intermittent connectivity. It introduces the first compressed sensing-based low-cost syndrome decoder for edge devices, enabling real-time error correction with significantly reduced computational overhead not addressed by previous low-overhead QEC frameworks.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop and experimentally validate an ion-chain-tailored topological quantum error correction (QEC) code that minimizes gate overhead and latency by explicitly exploiting trapped ion all-to-all connectivity and motional mode dynamics to improve logical error rates on NISQ trapped ion processors.

BACKGROUND: Current topological QEC codes such as the surface code are not optimized for trapped ion quantum processors, which feature slow entangling gates and unique motional mode couplings that introduce latency and excessive gate overhead. This inefficiency results in degraded error correction performance and limited error resilience on NISQ trapped ion systems. The open problem is designing QEC codes that align with the physical interaction patterns and timing constraints specific to ion chains to reduce cumulative gate errors and correction latency.

APPROACH: We propose a novel QEC code construction that integrates ion-chain-specific interaction graphs and motional mode coupling models to minimize entangling operations by dynamically scheduling gates to avoid contention and exploit native all-to-all connectivity. The method incorporates compiler-level optimizations that map logical qubits and syndrome extraction circuits onto ion chains with reduced gate count and temporal overhead. We will implement a tailored syndrome measurement protocol that leverages slower but high-fidelity Mølmer–Sørensen gates with optimized pulse shaping to reduce latency while preserving fault tolerance thresholds.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on benchmark trapped ion QEC datasets including the IonQ Public Quantum Teleportation Dataset, the Qiskit Trapped Ion Error Mitigation Dataset, and the Honeywell IonQ Error Syndrome Dataset. Baselines for comparison will include the standard Surface Code, the Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes (2026), and the Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler framework (2026). Evaluation metrics will measure logical error rate reduction (>30% improvement over Surface Code), gate count overhead reduction (>25% fewer two-qubit gates), and total syndrome extraction latency reduction (>20% faster). Experiments will use simulated ion chain hardware models with realistic noise profiles, running 50 independent noise realizations per configuration and conducting ablation studies on gate scheduling and connectivity exploitation.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior QEC codes optimized for generic qubit lattices or focusing on minimizing qubit count, this approach explicitly models and leverages the slow gate speeds, all-to-all connectivity, and motional mode couplings unique to trapped ion processors, resulting in concrete reductions in gate overhead and latency that prior topological codes and compiler frameworks do not achieve.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop a hardware-aware, adaptive quantum error suppression code framework optimized for highly variable noise profiles and stringent resource constraints in mobile NISQ quantum processors, enabling robust error correction with minimal qubit overhead.

BACKGROUND: Mobile quantum processors face unique challenges not addressed by existing QEC methods: dynamic, environment-induced noise variability and strict power/cooling limitations severely degrade the effectiveness of traditional QEC codes like the surface code which require high qubit overhead and static noise assumptions. Current low-overhead QEC frameworks fail to adapt in real-time to mobility-induced noise fluctuations, preventing reliable error correction on portable quantum devices.

APPROACH: We propose an adaptive quantum error correction scheme that integrates real-time hardware noise profiling with a novel family of ultra-low-overhead, hardware-aware error suppression codes specifically designed for mobile quantum processors. The method periodically characterizes qubit noise parameters using fast calibration circuits, then dynamically selects and tunes code parameters to match current noise conditions, optimizing logical error rates while maintaining minimal qubit and gate overhead. Key techniques include noise-tailored decoders, lightweight error syndrome extraction circuits, and on-chip feedback for fast code adaptation under power and cooling constraints.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate the framework on the IBM Quantum Experience Falcon and Rigetti Aspen mobile-prototype platforms, benchmarking against the Surface Code, the 10-qubit Low-Overhead QEC framework (2026), and QEC-aware compiler optimizations from “Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler Design” (2026). Metrics include logical qubit error rate (target <0.005), qubit overhead reduction (>50% vs. surface code), and real-time adaptability quantified as error rate stability improvement (>30% reduction in variance) under induced dynamic noise fluctuations. Experiments will run on 3 hardware instances with 5 random seeds each, including ablations on adaptive vs. static code selection.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior low-overhead or compiler-centric QEC approaches, this work uniquely combines real-time hardware noise profiling with dynamic error correction code adaptation tailored to the highly variable noise environments of mobile quantum processors, achieving robust logical error suppression at ultra-low overhead in resource-constrained, dynamic settings where static codes and traditional QEC fail.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop and experimentally validate an adaptive, low-overhead quantum error correction framework tailored for NISQ-era quantum data centers that dynamically balances qubit overhead and error suppression to optimize long-duration computational stability and throughput.

BACKGROUND: Current quantum error correction (QEC) codes for NISQ devices, such as surface codes, impose prohibitive qubit overhead and rigid error handling, limiting scalability and continuous operation in quantum data centers where noise profiles vary over time. Existing low-overhead QEC methods achieve moderate logical error rates but lack adaptivity to changing error landscapes and do not optimally trade off throughput with stability during extended runs.

APPROACH: We propose an adaptive QEC scheme combining a modular family of low-overhead codes (e.g., tailored variants of Bacon-Shor and [[4,2,2]] codes) integrated with real-time noise characterization feedback loops implemented via quantum tomography and machine learning predictors. This system dynamically adjusts code parameters—code distance, syndrome extraction frequency, and logical qubit encoding—based on detected noise fluctuations to minimize qubit overhead while maintaining target logical error rates and maximizing gate throughput. The approach includes co-design of quantum compiler optimizations to synergize with adaptive error correction.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate the proposed framework on IBM Quantum Falcon processors and Rigetti Aspen datasets, leveraging the Quantum Volume 32 and Google Sycamore noise profile datasets for benchmarking realistic noise models. Compare against surface codes, low-overhead QEC codes from “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum Devices” (2026), and error-resilient compiler baselines from “Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler Design” (2026). Metrics include logical error rate (target <0.005), qubit overhead reduction (>40% fewer physical qubits), and gate throughput improvement (>25% higher sustained logical gate execution rate) over continuous 12-hour simulated runs. Experiments will run with at least 5 seeds, include ablations on feedback frequency and code switching, and assume access to 50+ qubit NISQ devices with standard calibration tools.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior static or low-overhead QEC schemes, this work introduces a closed-loop, adaptive error correction system specifically designed for the variable noise environments of quantum data centers, enabling dynamic tradeoffs between overhead and stability that have not been demonstrated in prior frameworks, thus enabling scalable, long-duration quantum computing on NISQ hardware that current approaches cannot sustain.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a dynamic, multi-tenant aware quantum error correction compilation framework that integrates real-time noise characterization with adaptive low-overhead QEC code selection and qubit remapping to optimize logical error rates and throughput on NISQ devices operating under variable workloads.

BACKGROUND:  
NISQ devices suffer from high noise and hardware variability that limit the effectiveness of traditional QEC methods due to their large qubit overhead and static compilation approaches. Existing error-aware compilation frameworks like the Dynamic Error-Aware Compilation for Quantum Cloud Services do not incorporate adaptive QEC code selection or real-time noise fluctuations under multi-user conditions, restricting scalability and practical fault tolerance. The open problem is how to dynamically integrate error correction code adaptation, noise modeling, and qubit mapping at runtime to maintain low logical error rates without sacrificing throughput in shared NISQ environments.

APPROACH:  
We propose a dynamic compilation framework that continuously ingests real-time device noise metrics via Qiskit Ignis and IBM Quantum Experience device calibration data, and uses a reinforcement learning agent to select among multiple low-overhead QEC codes (e.g., tailored variants of surface codes, Bacon-Shor codes, and the low-overhead QEC codes from “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices” 2026) per workload. This is coupled with adaptive qubit remapping algorithms that optimize qubit placement to minimize correlated noise and gate error propagation. The system performs client-specific error-aware scheduling and code adaptation in a quantum cloud environment (simulated via IBM Quantum Cloud and Rigetti Aspen benchmarks), maintaining low compilation latency through heuristic pruning and approximate noise modeling. Ablation studies will isolate the impact of dynamic QEC selection versus static code deployment and the benefits of noise-informed qubit remapping.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate using:  
- Benchmark datasets: Quantum Volume 32 (QV32), IBM Qiskit Benchmark Suite (including Bernstein-Vazirani and Grover circuits), and Rigetti Aspen compiled workloads.  
- Baselines: Static Surface Code compilation (from “Limitations of QEC in NISQ systems” 2026), Error-Resilient Compiler from “Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler Design” (2026), and the Dynamic Error-Aware Compilation framework from the winning tournament.  
- Metrics: Achieve >30% reduction in logical error rates vs static surface code, reduce end-to-end compilation latency by ≥20% vs Dynamic Error-Aware Compilation baseline, and improve quantum workload throughput by ≥15% under multi-tenant conditions.  
- Protocol: Experiments will run on IBM’s 27-qubit Falcon processors and Rigetti Aspen-11 devices with 5 independent random seeds per workload. We will conduct detailed ablation studies on the effects of QEC code switching frequency and noise model granularity.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works that either statically deploy a single QEC code or perform error-aware compilation without integrated dynamic QEC adaptation, this approach uniquely combines runtime noise-informed QEC code selection, adaptive qubit remapping, and multi-tenant aware scheduling in a low-latency framework. This enables practical fault-tolerant operation on NISQ devices in variable noise and workload conditions, a capability not demonstrated in existing quantum compilation or QEC research to date.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a unified cost-efficiency scaling law framework that jointly optimizes data quantity, batch size, and model size under fixed cloud training time and budget constraints, explicitly incorporating pretraining data quality and downstream transfer impact to minimize total end-to-end cost for deploying Large Language Models.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws and cost-minimizing batch size techniques, such as those proposed in "Cost-Minimizing Batch Size and Model Scaling for Cloud Training Pipelines," optimize batch size and model capacity primarily for fixed compute or time budgets but treat pretraining data quantity and quality as fixed or secondary factors. This results in suboptimal decisions that overlook how dataset scale and composition affect cost-efficiency and downstream task performance. The open problem is to jointly model and optimize the interplay between pretraining data scale/quality, batch size, and model size under real cloud cost constraints to achieve the lowest total deployment cost while meeting performance targets.

APPROACH:  
We propose an integrated cost-efficiency scaling framework that extends existing compute-memory-cost scaling laws by explicitly incorporating multiple pretraining datasets of varying quality (e.g., C4, The Pile, and MassiveText), and introducing a parametric model of downstream performance as a function of data scale, batch size, and model size. Using inverse problem techniques, we learn this joint cost-performance landscape on cloud infrastructure cost models. Our method predicts optimal configurations (batch size, model size, and dataset sampling ratios) to minimize monetary cost for fixed training time budgets while ensuring target losses on pretraining and downstream tasks such as GLUE and SuperGLUE. This approach includes adaptive adjustment of batch size schedules during training to balance memory and compute efficiency dynamically.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate the framework using pretraining runs on C4, The Pile, and MassiveText datasets, with downstream evaluation on GLUE and SuperGLUE benchmarks. Baselines include GPT-3 (zero-shot), BERT-large, and the original Cost-Minimizing Batch Size and Model Scaling method. Metrics measured are final pretraining loss (target < 2.0 cross-entropy on C4), GLUE average score improvement (>3% absolute over baselines), and total cloud cost reduction (>15% lower than the baseline method) within fixed time budgets (e.g., 30 days on 64 A100 GPUs). Ablations will vary batch size schedules and dataset scale ratios across three seeds per configuration.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that optimizes batch size and model size independently and treats pretraining data scale as fixed, this approach jointly models and optimizes all three factors within a unified cost and downstream-performance framework under real cloud constraints. It is also the first to integrate dataset quality effects into cost-minimizing scaling laws and apply inverse problem solving to efficiently explore this multi-dimensional landscape, enabling more practical and cost-effective large-scale LLM deployments.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA: Develop a latency-constrained dynamic scaling framework that leverages adaptive layer pruning guided by scaling laws to optimize Large Language Model (LLM) inference on edge devices without sacrificing downstream task performance.

BACKGROUND: Current scaling laws for LLMs primarily focus on training efficiency and model size versus performance trade-offs but do not address runtime latency constraints on edge devices with limited compute. Existing adaptive pruning methods fail to guarantee strict latency budgets during inference, leading to unacceptable delays for real-time applications. There is a critical gap in bridging scaling laws with latency-constrained adaptive inference, especially for downstream task performance on resource-constrained hardware.

APPROACH: We propose a Latency-Constrained Adaptive Scaling (LCAS) method that dynamically prunes transformer layers at inference time based on a learned latency-performance trade-off model derived from empirical scaling laws. LCAS uses a lightweight controller trained to predict the minimal subset of layers needed to meet a strict latency cap (e.g., ≤50ms) on devices like Raspberry Pi 4, while preserving >90% baseline accuracy on downstream tasks. The approach integrates inverse problem formulations of scaling laws to inform pruning decisions, allowing adaptive model scaling aligned with latency budgets rather than static pruning or size-only compression.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate LCAS on GLUE, SuperGLUE, and SQuAD 2.0 benchmarks, comparing against BERT-base, GPT-3 (175B), and DistilBERT baselines. Metrics include task accuracy/F1 (>90% of baseline), inference latency (≤50ms target), and model size reduction (% FLOPs). Experiments run on Raspberry Pi 4 with 3 seeds each, including ablations on controller design and latency constraints.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior adaptive pruning that optimizes accuracy or model size without latency guarantees, LCAS explicitly incorporates scaling law insights to dynamically optimize transformer layer usage under strict latency constraints on edge devices, ensuring real-time performance without significant accuracy loss—an advancement not addressed by existing scaling law or pruning approaches.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA: Develop precise scaling laws for dynamic, adaptive large language model (LLM) capacity adjustment on resource-constrained edge devices by explicitly incorporating hardware constraints such as memory limits, power consumption, and latency into the scaling framework.

BACKGROUND: Existing scaling laws for large language models primarily consider model size, data quantity, and compute budget without accounting for strict hardware constraints encountered in edge devices. This gap limits the practical deployment of LLMs on edge platforms, which require real-time, energy-efficient inference under tight memory and power constraints. The open problem is how to formulate scaling laws that enable efficient, dynamic model capacity scaling on-device while balancing accuracy, latency, and hardware limits.

APPROACH: We propose an adaptive scaling framework that integrates hardware-specific constraints—memory footprint, power budget, and maximum allowable latency—directly into the scaling law equations governing model size, layer width/depth, and precision. Using lightweight on-device performance proxies and hardware telemetry, the model dynamically adjusts its architecture (e.g., width, depth, quantization level) in response to changing resource availability. We formalize these constraints mathematically to derive novel, multi-dimensional scaling laws that predict the optimal trade-off frontier between accuracy and resource consumption for edge deployment.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on GLUE and SuperGLUE benchmarks for natural language understanding, and SQuAD 2.0 for question answering to measure task performance under adaptive scaling. Baselines include BERT-base, DistilBERT, and EdgeBERT (lightweight edge-optimized LLM). Metrics: target >3% absolute F1 improvement over EdgeBERT at fixed latency ≤50ms on an ARM Cortex-A78 CPU, reduce peak memory usage by ≥25%, and maintain perplexity below 20 on WikiText-103. Experiments run on simulated and actual edge hardware with ≤8W power budget, across 5 random seeds, including ablations on individual hardware constraints (e.g., memory only, power only).

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that treats scaling laws as static functions of model and data scale (e.g., inverse problem approaches or theoretical formulations without hardware integration), our method explicitly incorporates dynamic hardware constraints into the scaling law framework, enabling real-time, efficient adaptive scaling on edge devices—a capability not addressed by existing scaling law research or zero-shot reasoning scaling analyses.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Leverage empirically derived, domain-specific scaling laws to dynamically optimize data augmentation volume and diversity thresholds for scientific text corpora, thereby maximizing downstream task performance and robustness without introducing degradation from synthetic or noisy data.

BACKGROUND:  
Current data augmentation methods for scientific NLP often degrade model performance in low-resource domains because they ignore how synthetic or noisy augmented data alters the underlying scaling behavior of language models. The open problem is to determine how much and what kind of augmented data should be incorporated to maximize signal extraction given the unique scaling dynamics of scientific corpora, preventing performance drops and improving robustness on downstream tasks.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel augmentation framework guided by scientifically validated scaling laws that relate model size, data volume, and data diversity to expected performance gains. By modeling the interaction between augmentation quantity/diversity and scaling curves specific to biomedical text (using inverse problem techniques and power-law fitting), we dynamically predict optimal augmentation parameters tailored to the target dataset. Augmentation is then applied selectively—balancing synthetic data volume and linguistic diversity—before fine-tuning large language models, ensuring augmentation stays within empirically optimal scaling thresholds.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on biomedical NLP benchmarks BioASQ, PubMedQA, and MedNLI. Baselines include BERT-base, BioBERT, and GPT-3 fine-tuned without scaling-guided augmentation. Metrics: F1 score (targeting >3% absolute improvement over baselines), robustness measured by performance stability under input perturbations (measured as <5% degradation), and perplexity on held-out scientific text (target <15). Experiments run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with three different random seeds per setting. Ablations will test impact of scaling law-guided volume control vs naïve augmentation and effect of diversity thresholds.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that applies augmentation heuristically or focuses on scaling laws for model size or compute, this method explicitly integrates domain-specific scaling laws to predict and enforce data augmentation regimes that prevent performance degradation. It is the first approach to quantitatively link augmentation parameters with scaling dynamics in low-resource scientific NLP, enabling principled augmentation rather than trial-and-error or uniform data boosting.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop precise, fine-grained scaling laws and a dynamic control algorithm enabling real-time adjustment of large language model (LLM) size on edge devices to simultaneously optimize accuracy, latency, and energy consumption under strict memory, compute, and battery constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Deploying LLMs on edge devices is hindered by limited memory, compute power, and battery life, necessitating aggressive model scaling. Existing scaling laws primarily focus on static or coarse-grained scaling and do not enable real-time, context-aware adaptation to diverse input complexities while balancing accuracy, latency, and energy use. This research addresses the open problem of deriving scalable, predictive laws and control mechanisms that permit continuous, data-dependent resizing of LLMs on constrained hardware without sacrificing user experience.

APPROACH:  
We propose to (1) empirically derive fine-grained, multi-dimensional scaling laws that relate model size, input complexity, and resource usage (memory, compute, energy) to accuracy and latency on edge hardware; (2) design a lightweight runtime controller that dynamically adjusts model parameters (e.g., layer widths, depths, or pruning patterns) in real time based on input difficulty and device state; and (3) integrate energy measurement feedback to optimize trade-offs explicitly, enabling model scaling decisions that minimize energy while meeting latency and accuracy targets.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be performed on SQuAD 2.0, GLUE, and WMT14 English-German translation datasets to cover diverse NLP tasks and input complexities. Baselines include BERT-base, DistilBERT, and MobileBERT for static scaling comparisons, and existing dynamic methods like early exiting (e.g., DeeBERT) and model pruning techniques (e.g., LoRA). Metrics include accuracy/F1 on SQuAD and GLUE (+≥2% absolute improvement over static scaled models), BLEU score on WMT14 translation (+≥1 point improvement), latency reduction (≥30% lower inference time vs static baselines), and energy consumption reduction (≥25% lower watt-seconds measured on representative edge processors). Experiments will be conducted on ARM-based edge devices with 3 seeds per configuration and ablation studies on controller design and input complexity metrics.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works that derive static or coarse-grained scaling laws or focus solely on accuracy or compute efficiency, this research formulates multi-objective, fine-grained scaling laws explicitly incorporating energy metrics and input complexity to enable real-time dynamic resizing on edge devices. This approach transcends existing methods’ limitations by delivering adaptive, context-aware control of LLM size that optimizes accuracy, latency, and energy simultaneously under severe hardware constraints.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a physics-informed stochastic differential equation (SDE) framework that integrates sparse single-cell cryo-EM data, environmental context, and AlphaFold-derived structural priors to jointly sample diverse protein folding pathways and transient intermediate ensembles, enabling accurate reconstruction and quantification of folding heterogeneity at the single-molecule level.

BACKGROUND:  
Current protein structure prediction methods, including AlphaFold2, excel at predicting static native conformations but do not capture the dynamic folding process or transient intermediates. Existing folding pathway models either require dense time-series data or fail to scale to high-dimensional folding landscapes and sparse single-cell measurements, limiting insight into folding heterogeneity and environment-specific folding variability. There is an unmet need for generative models that can probabilistically infer multiple folding trajectories from sparse single-cell structural snapshots while incorporating physical constraints and environmental factors.

APPROACH:  
We propose “PathFold-SDE,” a generative latent-variable model where folding dynamics are modeled as stochastic differential equations conditioned on physical priors (e.g., energy landscapes from molecular force fields) and enhanced with AlphaFold-derived residue contact and distance priors. Sparse single-cell cryo-EM intermediates and environmental metadata (e.g., pH, chaperone presence) serve as partial observations in a variational inference framework, enabling posterior sampling of multiple folding pathways and transient states. The model leverages amortized inference with neural SDE solvers to efficiently sample diverse trajectories consistent with observed intermediates and environment. AlphaFold’s structural embeddings guide the latent space, improving fidelity and biological plausibility under sparse data regimes.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark datasets: (1) Single-particle cryo-EM folding intermediate datasets for small proteins from the EMPIAR database (e.g., EMPIAR-10181), (2) Folding kinetics and intermediate structural data from the Protein Folding Database (PFDB), and (3) Single-molecule Förster resonance energy transfer (smFRET) time-series datasets with environmental annotations. Baselines include (a) AlphaFold2 static predictions, (b) Stochastic Folding Pathway Sampling with Sparse Single-Cell Inputs (the winning tournament method), and (c) Molecular Dynamics simulations enhanced with Markov State Models (MSMs). Metrics: improve pathway coverage by >20% over baseline on likelihood of observed intermediates, achieve >15% better agreement with experimentally measured intermediate population distributions (e.g., via Earth Mover’s Distance), and reduce computational sampling cost by 30% relative to MSMs. Perform ablation on inclusion of AlphaFold priors and environmental conditioning. Training runs on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, 5 random seeds per experiment.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods that either predict static native structures (AlphaFold) or sample folding pathways without leveraging learned structural priors and environmental context, PathFold-SDE integrates AlphaFold-informed latent structural representations with physics-based stochastic dynamics conditioned on sparse single-cell data and environment. This approach uniquely enables tractable, probabilistic sampling of diverse folding routes and transient intermediates reflecting folding heterogeneity, which existing models cannot achieve due to data sparsity, lack of environmental modeling, or inability to incorporate deep learned priors.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a unified probabilistic framework that integrates AlphaFold predictions with sparse, noisy experimental data (low-resolution cryo-EM, limited cross-linking MS, and sparse NMR restraints) via advanced uncertainty-aware multimodal fusion and hierarchical Bayesian denoising to produce robust, accurate protein structure models under highly incomplete input conditions.

BACKGROUND:  
Current state-of-the-art integrative modeling methods like DeepTracer-LowResEnhance and conventional AlphaFold struggle to effectively incorporate uncertain, sparse, and heterogeneous experimental data such as low-resolution cryo-EM maps, sparse cross-linking mass spectrometry, and limited NMR-derived distance restraints. The core challenge is the lack of principled uncertainty modeling and noise filtering during data fusion, which leads to ambiguous and often inaccurate structural reconstructions in challenging experimental contexts where high-resolution data are unavailable.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical Bayesian fusion framework where AlphaFold-generated structural priors are probabilistically combined with multiple sparse experimental modalities, each modeled with explicit noise and uncertainty distributions. Key components include: (1) modality-specific noise models and uncertainty quantification learned from benchmark datasets, (2) a Bayesian denoising step that iteratively refines experimental data likelihoods, and (3) a joint posterior sampling approach that integrates predicted and experimental constraints to generate an ensemble of plausible structures with calibrated confidence scores. This approach leverages variational inference and Markov Chain Monte Carlo to handle uncertainty propagation and missing data jointly, enabling robust structure prediction from noisy, incomplete inputs.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark datasets: (1) EMDataBank’s low-resolution cryo-EM maps (e.g., EMDB entries with resolution >6Å), (2) the XL-MS benchmark from PRIDE repository with known protein complexes, and (3) the Protein NMR Restraints Database with sparse distance restraints. Baselines include AlphaFold2, DeepTracer-LowResEnhance, and Integrative Modeling Platform (IMP). Metrics: improve TM-score by >5% absolute over baselines on low-res datasets; reduce structural RMSD by >1.0 Å on XL-MS restrained complexes; increase Q-factor for NMR restraint satisfaction by >10%. Experiments will use NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a training budget of 300 GPU-hours, performing 5 independent runs per dataset plus ablation studies isolating noise models and uncertainty quantification components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike AlphaFold or DeepTracer-LowResEnhance, which treat experimental data as fixed constraints or perform limited denoising, our method explicitly models uncertainty and noise probabilistically within a unified Bayesian framework and integrates multiple sparse modalities simultaneously. This principled uncertainty-aware fusion enables more accurate and reliable protein structure predictions from highly incomplete or noisy experimental data, a capability absent in prior works.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hybrid modeling framework that fuses AlphaFold2 structural predictions with time-resolved experimental datasets—specifically single-molecule FRET (smFRET), hydrogen-deuterium exchange mass spectrometry (HDX-MS), and time-resolved cryo-EM—to generate dynamic ensembles of protein conformations capturing functional motions and transient states.

BACKGROUND:  
Current state-of-the-art protein structure predictors like AlphaFold2 deliver highly accurate static structures but cannot model protein conformational dynamics or transient states critical for function and allosteric regulation. Experimental techniques such as smFRET, HDX-MS, and time-resolved cryo-EM provide complementary temporal and spatial insights but lack integrative modeling frameworks that combine these heterogeneous data with AI-based predictions. This gap prevents comprehensive, time-resolved structural characterization of proteins essential for drug design targeting dynamic allosteric sites.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-modal spatiotemporal integration framework that (1) uses AlphaFold2-generated static models as structural priors; (2) incorporates smFRET distance distributions and HDX-MS protection factors as dynamic constraints encoded via differentiable loss terms; and (3) leverages time-resolved cryo-EM density maps to refine conformational ensembles through deep generative models like variational autoencoders or normalizing flows trained jointly on these data. The framework outputs probabilistic ensembles reflecting conformational dynamics across experimentally observed timescales. Model training employs end-to-end differentiable optimization to reconcile AI predictions with experimental observables, enabling capture of transient and intermediate states.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will benchmark on the following datasets: (1) the Protein Ensemble Database (PED) for dynamic structural ensembles; (2) published smFRET datasets on proteins such as adenylate kinase and calmodulin; and (3) time-resolved cryo-EM datasets from the Electron Microscopy Public Image Archive (EMPIAR) involving dynamic complexes like the spliceosome. Baselines include static AlphaFold2 models, DeepTracer-LowResEnhance, and HDX-guided ensemble modeling tools such as HDXer. Evaluation metrics include: (a) ensemble RMSD reduction by >1.5 Å compared to AlphaFold2 alone, (b) improved agreement with experimental FRET efficiency distributions quantified by Kolmogorov-Smirnov test (target p-value < 0.01 improvement), and (c) increased correlation (>10% absolute increase in Pearson’s r) between predicted and observed HDX-MS protection factors. Experimental protocol includes training on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of approximately 2,000 GPU hours, performing 5 independent runs with different random seeds, plus ablation studies removing each experimental modality to quantify their contribution.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike previous methods that focus on static structures or single data modalities, our approach uniquely enables end-to-end fusion of AI-based predictions with multiple heterogeneous time-resolved experimental datasets, generating dynamic conformational ensembles rather than fixed models. Prior work such as DeepTracer-LowResEnhance improves static low-resolution maps but does not capture protein dynamics, and HDX-guided ensemble methods do not integrate AI structural priors or time-resolved cryo-EM, limiting their temporal and spatial resolution.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a multi-modal transformer architecture that integrates heterogeneous experimental datasets with cross-modal attention to resolve ambiguity and improve accuracy in large protein assembly structures, demonstrated on challenging multi-resolution cryo-EM and X-ray data.

BACKGROUND:  
Current protein structure prediction methods, including AlphaFold2 and recent multimodal approaches, struggle to jointly process and reconcile diverse, high-volume experimental data such as cryo-EM maps at varying resolutions, X-ray crystallography, and cross-linking mass spectrometry. This results in ambiguous or inconsistent models, especially for large multi-subunit complexes like the 26S proteasome. The open problem is to design a scalable model that dynamically weighs conflicting signals from heterogeneous input modalities to produce consistent, high-fidelity structural predictions for large assemblies.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-modal transformer model with specialized cross-modal attention layers that ingest raw and processed inputs from cryo-EM density maps (low to high resolution), X-ray crystallography electron density, and cross-linking/mass spectrometry constraints. The model explicitly learns to weigh and reconcile conflicting data by attending across modalities and resolution scales during end-to-end training. The architecture incorporates hierarchical encoder blocks for each modality followed by a unified cross-modal transformer decoder that outputs residue-level structural embeddings and assembly configurations. An uncertainty-aware loss function penalizes inconsistent predictions across data sources to enforce model consistency.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will benchmark on three named datasets: EMDataBank’s 26S proteasome cryo-EM maps at multiple resolutions, the Protein Data Bank’s multi-subunit complexes with matched X-ray and cross-linking data (e.g., ribosome structures PDB IDs 4V6F, 5U9F), and the Cryo-EM Map Challenge dataset. Baselines for comparison include AlphaFold2 multimers, DeepTracer-LowResEnhance (2024), and Multi-State Modeling via CryoDRGN. Metrics include >3% absolute improvement on global TM-score, >4% improvement on local lDDT-Cα scores, and a ≥10% reduction in structural ambiguity quantified by ensemble entropy measures. Experiments will run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs over 5 random seeds, with ablations on cross-modal attention and uncertainty loss components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike DeepTracer-LowResEnhance and existing multi-modal methods that either focus on single resolution cryo-EM or treat experimental data independently, our model jointly processes heterogeneous, conflicting multi-resolution data streams with dynamic cross-modal attention, enabling scalable, end-to-end resolution of structural ambiguities in very large protein assemblies. This approach explicitly addresses inconsistent inputs, which prior work cannot reconcile effectively at scale.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a compressed, diversity-aware generative transformer framework that enables real-time, on-device iterative protein structure design and refinement by integrating low-latency inference with adaptive feedback from experimental Cryo-EM and stability data.

BACKGROUND:  
Current generative models for protein design, including state-of-the-art transformer-based systems, require large cloud-based resources and exhibit slow inference times, preventing real-time iterative design directly at synthetic biology labs or portable settings. While AlphaFold excels in high-accuracy protein structure prediction, it is computationally intensive and not optimized for on-device, closed-loop design cycles that use experimental low-resolution Cryo-EM data or stability feedback to refine designs dynamically. This bottleneck delays experimental turnaround and limits rapid prototyping of novel proteins.

APPROACH:  
We propose a lightweight, pruned and quantized generative transformer distilled from large protein design models, augmented with a novel diversity-aware loss function to preserve structural novelty despite compression. We extend this by integrating a feedback loop that inputs experimental low-resolution Cryo-EM maps (e.g., from EMDB) and experimental stability measurements (e.g., ProTherm dataset) to iteratively refine design candidates on-device. The model leverages multi-modal embeddings combining sequence, predicted structure, and experimental cues to guide generation, all optimized for sub-second inference latency on Raspberry Pi-class hardware.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will use Foldit benchmark tasks, EMDB low-resolution Cryo-EM maps, and ProTherm stability change datasets. Baselines include full-size generative transformers (e.g., ProteinMPNN), AlphaFold2, and DeepTracer-LowResEnhance. Metrics include inference latency (<1s per design), structural novelty (measured by TM-score diversity >0.1 over baseline), and design stability prediction accuracy (RMSE <0.5 kcal/mol on ProTherm). Experiments will run on Raspberry Pi 4 hardware with 5 independent seeds and ablations on pruning ratios and feedback integration components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior compressed models that only optimize latency or structural diversity, this approach uniquely couples on-device protein design with iterative refinement driven by real experimental Cryo-EM and stability data, enabling closed-loop, real-time design beyond static prediction capabilities of AlphaFold and existing generative models limited to cloud environments.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA: Develop a privacy-preserving, missingness-aware causal representation learning framework that produces interpretable causal graphs tailored for large-scale, heterogeneous healthcare observational data, enabling trustworthy and regulation-compliant causal discovery in clinical batch analytics.

BACKGROUND: Current causal discovery methods in healthcare fail to simultaneously handle heterogeneous multimodal data with missingness, ensure patient privacy compliance, and yield interpretable causal models aligned with healthcare regulatory standards. This limits their practical utility for clinicians and policymakers who require transparent, actionable insights from observational healthcare datasets often characterized by strict privacy constraints and complex, nonlinear causal interactions.

APPROACH: We propose a novel framework that integrates (1) a privacy-preserving causal representation learner leveraging federated learning and differential privacy to protect sensitive health data, (2) a missingness-aware structural causal model that explicitly models MNAR (Missing Not At Random) mechanisms within the causal graph, and (3) an interpretable causal graph extraction module based on sparse, disentangled latent variable disentanglement aligned with domain ontologies to boost clinical interpretability. Optimization uses a constrained continuous search over nonlinear causal graphs employing an adapted KiNCD backbone enhanced with healthcare-specific priors and compliance constraints.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on heterogeneous healthcare datasets including MIMIC-IV (critical care EHR), UK Biobank (longitudinal multimodal biobank data), and eICU Collaborative Research Database for batch causal discovery benchmarking. Baselines include KiNCD, S-FCI, and Orthogonal Structure Search. Metrics: >5% absolute improvement in Structural Hamming Distance (SHD), >10% better F1 score for edge identification, and quantifiable interpretability via human expert evaluation (clinical domain expert-rated causal model transparency score). Use a protocol of 5 independent random seeds, training on GPUs with a 48-hour compute budget, and conduct ablation studies on privacy and missingness modules.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior methods (e.g., KiNCD, S-FCI) that do not address missing not at random data or privacy constraints, and unlike existing interpretable evaluation frameworks, our approach jointly solves privacy, missingness, and interpretability challenges in large-scale heterogeneous healthcare data, producing actionable and regulation-compliant causal models validated by domain experts, enabling real-world clinical deployment.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a robust invariant causal mechanism extraction framework that explicitly models and corrects for heterogeneity in multi-hospital healthcare observational data by integrating domain-adaptive nonlinear causal discovery with principled missing data handling and confounder adjustment to identify stable causal relations transferable across diverse clinical environments.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing causal discovery methods fail to reliably identify stable causal mechanisms across heterogeneous healthcare datasets due to varying patient demographics, measurement protocols, and missingness patterns inherent in multi-hospital data. This heterogeneity induces domain shifts and complex confounding that cause prior methods to produce unstable or non-generalizable causal graphs, limiting their clinical utility for trustworthy decision support. The open problem is how to extract invariant causal relations that remain valid across hospitals despite domain-specific biases, nonlinear dependencies, and pervasive data quality issues.

APPROACH:  
We propose a unified framework that combines (1) a domain-adaptive nonlinear causal discovery module inspired by KiNCD’s Kolmogorov-Arnold Network integration to flexibly model complex causal functions, (2) an invariant risk minimization (IRM)-based regularizer to enforce stability of discovered mechanisms across hospital domains, and (3) a novel missing data imputation and bias correction procedure leveraging hospital-specific metadata alongside causal graph structure to adjust for heterogeneous missingness and confounders. The method explicitly learns hospital domain embeddings to model domain shifts and integrates these into both the causal structure learning and confounding adjustment phases, enabling extraction of causal relations invariant to hospital-specific data generation processes.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on multi-hospital clinical datasets with known causal benchmarks such as MIMIC-III (critical care data from Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center), eICU Collaborative Research Database (multi-center ICU data), and the UK Biobank cardiovascular subset. Baselines include KiNCD, S-FCI, Orthogonal Structure Search, and a state-of-the-art multi-environment causal discovery method based on IRM. Metrics: structural Hamming distance (SHD) improvement >15% over baselines in recovering ground-truth edges, stability measured by inter-domain causal consistency score improved by >20%, and downstream clinical outcome prediction AUC uplift >3%. Experiments run with 5 random seeds, ablations for missing data handling, domain embedding removal, and IRM regularizer strength, on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with training budgets up to 48 hours per run.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike KiNCD and S-FCI which do not explicitly model domain shifts or heterogeneous missingness, and prior IRM-based causal discovery methods that assume simpler linear or i.i.d. settings, our approach uniquely integrates nonlinear causal discovery with principled domain adaptation and bias correction tailored for heterogeneous, missing, and confounded multi-hospital healthcare data. This enables robust, invariant causal mechanism extraction that generalizes across real-world clinical environments, a capability absent from existing SOTA methods.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a physics- and causality-informed nonlinear causal discovery framework that integrates spatiotemporal PDE constraints with Kolmogorov-Arnold Network-based structure learning to robustly infer physically consistent causal graphs from high-dimensional climate observational data.

BACKGROUND:  
Current causal discovery methods for high-dimensional observational climate data fail to jointly enforce physical laws and nonlinear causal relationships, resulting in learned causal graphs that are either physically inconsistent or lack robustness to spatiotemporal dependencies. Existing physics-informed generative counterfactual methods address intervention modeling but do not solve the foundational challenge of discovering causal structure from purely observational data while respecting PDE-based climate dynamics. This gap limits the scientific and policy value of causal inference in climate and environmental sciences.

APPROACH:  
We propose Physics-Integrated Kolmogorov-Arnold Network Causal Discovery (PI-KANCD), a hybrid framework that incorporates known PDE constraints governing climate processes directly into a nonlinear causal discovery model based on the KAN architecture. PI-KANCD uses a differentiable physics loss enforcing spatiotemporal consistency alongside a continuous optimization objective that uncovers causal graphs from observational data. By jointly optimizing the causal graph structure and embedding physical PDE constraints, PI-KANCD ensures the discovered causal relationships respect both nonlinear dependencies and fundamental climate dynamics. This approach enables the identification of latent confounders and nonlinear causal mechanisms while maintaining physical realism.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate PI-KANCD on the CMIP6 climate simulation dataset, ERA5 reanalysis data, and the NOAA Global Historical Climatology Network (GHCN) observational dataset. Compare against KiNCD, S-FCI, and Orthogonal Structure Search baselines using Structural Hamming Distance (SHD), F1-score for edge recovery, and PDE residual error to quantify physical consistency. Target >5% absolute improvement in F1-score over KiNCD and reduce PDE residual error by at least 30% relative to unconstrained methods. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with five random seeds and include ablation studies isolating the effect of PDE constraint integration.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike KiNCD and existing nonlinear causal discovery methods, PI-KANCD uniquely integrates explicit PDE constraints within the causal structure learning process, ensuring physically consistent causal graphs in spatiotemporal settings. Prior physics-informed generative models do not address causal discovery from observational data, while existing causal discovery algorithms do not enforce physical laws, making PI-KANCD the first to unify these capabilities for high-dimensional climate data.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA: Develop a Federated Distributed Variational Autoencoder (Fed-DVAE) framework that enables privacy-preserving, scalable causal discovery from multi-cohort, observational genomic data by jointly learning consensus latent causal graphs across federated nodes without centralizing raw data.

BACKGROUND: Existing scalable causal discovery methods like Distributed Variational Autoencoders successfully handle millions of genomic variables by partitioning data across compute nodes but require centralized data aggregation, which is infeasible for sensitive genomic datasets spanning multiple cohorts due to privacy and regulatory constraints. There is a critical need for methods that can scale causal discovery to population-level genomic data distributed across institutions while preserving privacy, enabling discovery of complex gene-environment causal mechanisms in a federated, multi-domain setting.

APPROACH: We design Fed-DVAE, a novel distributed variational autoencoder architecture with consensus latent inference via federated averaging and secure aggregation protocols. Each cohort node trains a local VAE on its partitioned genomic observational data, encoding latent causal representations and causal edges. Periodically, encrypted model parameters and latent graph embeddings are aggregated centrally to update a global consensus model without sharing raw data. The framework incorporates nonlinear causal mechanism parameterization using Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) modules to capture complex gene-environment interactions and uses orthogonal structure search constraints to improve causal graph identifiability. Fed-DVAE supports asynchronous updates and scales linearly with up to 100 federated nodes.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate Fed-DVAE on the UK Biobank genomic dataset, the 1000 Genomes Project dataset, and the GTEx multi-tissue expression dataset, measuring causal edge recall (target >70%), reconstruction error (target <0.05 MSE), and scalability (linear speedup up to 100 nodes). Baseline comparisons include Distributed Variational Autoencoders (DVAEs), KiNCD, and Orthogonal Structure Search methods. Experiments will run on a compute cluster with 100 GPU nodes, with 5 random seeds per setting and ablations on KAN integration, federated update frequency, and privacy-preserving aggregation techniques.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior distributed causal discovery methods that require centralized data and cannot handle privacy constraints, Fed-DVAE enables federated learning of latent causal graphs across multiple genomic cohorts without raw data exchange. Integration of KAN-based nonlinear causal modeling and orthogonal structure search into a federated VAE framework for scalable, privacy-preserving causal discovery from observational data is unprecedented and enables new opportunities for population-scale genomic causal inference.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable, interpretable latent confounder disentanglement framework for nonlinear causal discovery in large-scale, multimodal epidemiological datasets with heterogeneous subpopulations and missing data, leveraging a novel hierarchical variational autoencoder integrated with invariant risk minimization and graph neural causal structure learning.

BACKGROUND:  
Current causal discovery methods for population-level epidemiological studies struggle to robustly identify causal factors across diverse subpopulations from large-scale observational data that is multimodal and incomplete. Existing approaches either do not scale well to millions of samples or fail to disentangle latent confounders that vary across subgroups, limiting reliable causal inference necessary for targeted public health interventions. The open problem is designing a method that jointly handles heterogeneity, missingness, nonlinear causal dependencies, and scalability while preserving interpretability of latent confounders across populations.

APPROACH:  
We propose Hierarchical Latent Invariant Causal Discovery (HLICD), which jointly learns subgroup-specific latent confounders via a hierarchical variational autoencoder (HVAE) that encodes multimodal epidemiological features and missing data patterns. HLICD enforces invariance of causal mechanisms across subpopulations using invariant risk minimization (IRM) principles and integrates a graph neural network-based causal structure learner to capture nonlinear causal relationships among observed and latent variables. Missing data are handled natively in the HVAE through learned data masking and imputation layers, facilitating end-to-end training on heterogeneous data. The resulting latent representations disentangle subgroup-specific confounding factors that maintain interpretability through sparsity and disentanglement regularizers.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate HLICD on large-scale population-level epidemiological datasets including the UK Biobank, NHANES (National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey), and All of Us Research Program datasets. Compare against baselines: KiNCD, S-FCI, and Orthogonal Structure Search. Metrics will include Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) for causal graph accuracy (target >15% absolute improvement over baselines), AUC-PR and F1-score for latent confounder identification, and runtime scalability benchmarks on GPU clusters (aiming to reduce training time by 30%). Perform ablation studies on hierarchical latent structure, IRM regularization, and missing data handling components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike KiNCD or S-FCI, HLICD explicitly models and disentangles heterogeneous latent confounders across subpopulations at population scale with a hierarchical latent structure, enabling robust causal discovery in the presence of multimodal missing data. Prior methods do not jointly leverage HVAE-based subgroup latent factor disentanglement with invariant risk minimization and graph neural causal learners, nor scale efficiently to millions of epidemiological records while preserving interpretability of latent confounders.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Develop scalable, aging-aware analog in-memory computing (AIMC) accelerators for energy-efficient neuromorphic workloads in data centers that integrate fault-tolerant reliability mechanisms and seamless analog-digital interfacing to sustain performance over device lifetime.

BACKGROUND:  
Current analog in-memory computing accelerators for neuromorphic computing excel in energy efficiency but fail to maintain reliable, high-throughput operation at scale in data center environments due to device aging, variability, and lack of integrated fault-tolerance. Prior work in neuromorphic synaptic devices focuses on low-power or high-precision components but does not address scalable, long-term energy-efficient analog computation in large-scale, batch-processing settings or integrate these within existing digital data center infrastructures.

APPROACH:  
We propose a modular AIMC accelerator architecture combining multilevel, aging-tolerant synaptic devices with adaptive calibration and in-situ aging detection circuits. The system uses hierarchical redundancy and error-correcting codes tailored for analog weight drift, coupled with mixed-signal interfaces enabling transparent analog-digital co-processing. Aging-aware training algorithms update synaptic weights considering device degradation profiles, while run-time monitoring adjusts operation dynamically to maintain energy efficiency and computational accuracy over extended device lifetimes.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on large-scale neuromorphic workloads using the ImageNet-1K classification dataset, the COCO object detection dataset, and the Penn Treebank language modeling dataset to test vision and sequence learning. Baselines will include ResNet-50 on digital accelerators, the ferroelectric synaptic transistor DropConnect system (2025), and the all-2D materials artificial synapse (2021). Key metrics are >2% absolute improvement in top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1K compared to baseline AIMC systems, a reduction in energy consumption by at least 30% vs. hardware-implemented DropConnect, and latency reduction of 25% over the all-2D materials device. Experimental protocol includes training and inference on AIMC simulators with hardware-in-the-loop aging emulation over 3 independent runs, with ablation studies on redundancy schemes and aging-aware calibration.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike existing neuromorphic devices and analog accelerators that optimize only for energy or precision at small scale and short term, this work meaningfully extends AIMC technology to large-scale, fault-tolerant, and aging-resilient operation in data center contexts with integrated analog-digital interfacing—enabling practical deployment of energy-efficient neuromorphic computing beyond prototype devices or embedded applications.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Design a hierarchical memristive array architecture with integrated adaptive on-chip variability compensation and real-time energy-aware workload management to enable ultra-low-power, calibration-free neuromorphic computing for edge IoT devices.

BACKGROUND:  
Memristive arrays for neuromorphic computing face severe device-to-device variability necessitating costly, energy-inefficient offline calibration, limiting deployment in resource-constrained edge IoT sensors and shortening system lifetime. Existing variability compensation methods lack continuous adaptation and energy-aware operational control, causing either accuracy degradation or excessive area/power overhead, constraining practical edge applications.

APPROACH:  
We propose a modular hierarchical memristive crossbar array architecture embedding lightweight, adaptive variability compensation circuits at multiple granularity levels (cell, tile, array) to dynamically correct device-induced errors during operation without external calibration. Coupled with an on-chip energy-aware workload manager that monitors power consumption and dynamically throttles or reallocates neuromorphic computations, this system maintains accuracy within 2% of ideal calibrated arrays while reducing average power by >20%. Variability compensators employ closed-loop feedback using low-overhead sense amplifiers and digital correction codes, enabling continuous self-calibration. The workload manager leverages real-time power monitoring via integrated sensors to optimize energy consumption under variable environmental and workload conditions.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on WISDM IoT Sensor Dataset, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10 to benchmark sensor-domain and vision-domain neuromorphic workloads. Baselines include Compact Calibration-Free Memristive Array Architecture with Variability Compensation (tournament winner), Hardware-Implemented DropConnect Function (2025), and Low-Power Vertical Dual-Gate Neurotransistor (2023). Metrics: accuracy drop under 2% vs fully calibrated arrays, area overhead below 10%, power reduction ≥20% vs tournament winner, and inference latency reduction ≥15%. Experimental protocol includes 5 independent runs with random device variability models, ablation studies isolating hierarchical compensation and workload management, and deployment assumptions targeting edge IoT-class hardware with ≤100 mW power budget.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that applies static or single-level variability compensation (e.g., lightweight on-chip correction without workload adaptation), our hierarchical multi-granularity approach enables finer control and error correction with minimal overhead. Additionally, integrating real-time energy-aware workload management directly into the memristive array system addresses power-performance tradeoffs dynamically, a capability absent in current memristive neuromorphic architectures, enabling robust, energy-efficient operation in unpredictable edge environments.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Develop a hybrid neuromorphic architecture combining adaptive event-driven sparse spiking networks with energy-aware optoelectronic synapses and hardware-implemented stochastic DropConnect to achieve ultra-low power, high-accuracy wearable health monitoring under real-world noisy biosignal conditions.

BACKGROUND: Wearable health monitors require neuromorphic systems that can detect intermittent, noisy biosignals with high sensitivity while adhering to stringent energy budgets. Existing solutions either sacrifice detection accuracy for power savings or consume excessive energy to maintain sensitivity, and none effectively integrate adaptive sparse coding with advanced synaptic device technologies and stochastic regularization to optimize this tradeoff under realistic conditions.

APPROACH: We propose a co-designed system featuring (1) adaptive sparse coding algorithms that dynamically tune spiking activity based on signal statistics to minimize unnecessary firing, (2) integration of high-precision multibit optoelectronic synapses based on 2D vdW heterostructures for low-power, high-bandwidth synaptic weight updates, and (3) hardware-implemented ferroelectric synaptic transistors with stochastic DropConnect functionality to improve network robustness and energy efficiency without software overhead. These components are combined into an event-driven spiking network optimized for real-time biosignal detection with continuous self-calibration.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on PhysioNet MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Dataset, CAP Sleep Database, and the MIMIC-III waveform subset to test arrhythmia and sleep apnea event detection; compare against baselines including traditional convolutional neuromorphic networks (from 2016), OEFGT-based synaptic arrays (2025), and ferroelectric stochastic DropConnect networks (2025). Metrics: target >5% absolute improvement in detection F1-score, >40% reduction in average energy per inference, and >30% reduction in latency versus best baseline. Experimental protocol includes training on a fixed compute budget equivalent to 100 GPU-hours, hardware simulation on a 28nm CMOS process with device-level models, 5 random seeds per condition, and ablation studies isolating each architectural component.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that addresses energy efficiency, synaptic device innovation, or stochastic regularization in isolation, this research uniquely combines adaptive sparse coding with advanced multibit optoelectronic synapses and hardware DropConnect in a unified system targeted at the stringent, dynamic constraints of wearable health monitoring, enabling a practical solution that prior neuromorphic architectures and devices cannot achieve.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Develop and evaluate a novel hierarchical communication protocol combined with adaptive network topologies to drastically reduce inter-core synchronization overhead and communication energy in large-scale event-driven spiking neuromorphic data centers.

BACKGROUND: Large-scale neuromorphic data centers face a critical bottleneck in inter-core communication and synchronization overhead that severely limits scalability and energy efficiency. Existing event-driven spiking network solutions focus primarily on low-power edge devices and do not adequately address the challenges of distributed parallelism, high throughput, and low-latency synchronization required at data center scale.

APPROACH: We propose a hierarchical, locality-aware communication protocol that dynamically clusters neuromorphic cores based on spike traffic patterns to minimize costly global synchronizations. This is paired with a flexible reconfigurable network topology leveraging sparse inter-cluster links and energy-aware routing to balance throughput and synchronization cost. The approach utilizes hardware-software co-design to implement low-latency event aggregation and prioritization mechanisms at the communication layer, reducing spike congestion and synchronization stalls.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate on the N-MNIST, DVS128 Gesture, and CIFAR-10-DVS event-based neuromorphic datasets using baseline models such as TrueNorth, Loihi 2, and SpiNNaker systems. Metrics include energy per inference (targeting >40% reduction vs Loihi 2), end-to-end latency (aiming for >30% latency reduction), and throughput (measured as spikes/sec/core, targeting at least 25% improvement). Experimental setups will use FPGA-based neuromorphic testbeds and simulators, with training compute budgets capped at 500 GPU hours, conducting five random seeds for statistical significance and ablation studies on protocol layers and topology configurations.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work focusing on device-level energy efficiency (e.g., 2D material synapses or neurotransistors) or edge-optimized event-driven networks, this research uniquely targets the systemic communication and synchronization bottleneck in large-scale neuromorphic data centers by combining hierarchical adaptive communication protocols with topology reconfiguration — capabilities not addressed by existing hardware or protocol solutions.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Develop a memory- and energy-efficient online spike decoding architecture leveraging adaptive temporal sparsity compression and event-driven neuromorphic hardware co-design to enable sub-256 KB RAM deployment for ultra-low power wearable applications with improved reconstruction accuracy and latency.

BACKGROUND: Existing spike decoding architectures for neuromorphic wearables demand large buffer memory to reconstruct signals, which is impractical for small form factor devices constrained to less than 256 KB RAM. This limits deployment in ultra-low power wearables due to excessive memory usage and energy overhead, and existing decoders struggle to balance memory footprint, accuracy, and real-time operation. There is a need for an online decoding solution that compresses temporal spike data adaptively while minimizing memory and energy consumption without sacrificing reconstruction fidelity or processing latency.

APPROACH: We propose an adaptive temporal sparsity compression algorithm that dynamically identifies and encodes temporal spike event patterns with variable-length representations to minimize buffer size. This will be integrated with an event-driven neuromorphic hardware co-design that performs incremental online decoding using ultra-low power mixed-signal circuits, eliminating large intermediate storage. The architecture supports sublinear memory growth relative to input length and incorporates error-correcting feedback loops to maintain >90% signal reconstruction accuracy. Implementation targets deployment on wearable-class neuromorphic processors with less than 256 KB RAM and power budgets under 50 µW.

EXPERIMENT: Validate using the Wearable Activity Dataset, the NeuroTycho dataset (monkey motor cortex recordings), and the CHB-MIT Scalp EEG database to evaluate performance on diverse physiological signals. Compare against the baseline decoders: the original Memory-Constrained Spike Decoding method (tournament winner), the Convolutional Spike Decoder (2016), and the Hardware DropConnect Synaptic Decoder (2025). Quantitative metrics include signal reconstruction accuracy (target >92%), memory usage reduction (>50% less than baseline), and end-to-end decoding latency (reduce by ≥30%). Experiments will run on simulated neuromorphic hardware with power modeling, using 5 random seeds, and include ablations on compression granularity and feedback loop complexity.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that passively exploits temporal sparsity or relies on static decoding buffers, this method dynamically compresses spike events with adaptive coding integrated tightly with event-driven hardware to enable ultra-low memory and power operation with improved reconstruction accuracy and latency. It surpasses the Memory-Constrained Spike Decoding baseline by achieving higher accuracy at lower memory and lower latency, while existing neuromorphic decoders do not address memory constraints in wearables or co-design for memory-driven decoding efficiency.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable, reinforcement learning-driven cloud-native platform for continuous high-throughput molecular generation and real-time multi-assay biological feedback integration, enabling rapid industrial-scale drug candidate screening and lead optimization.

BACKGROUND:  
Current generative AI methods for drug discovery struggle to efficiently handle the massive data throughput and diverse real-time biological assay feedback required for industrial-scale drug screening. This limitation restricts the ability to generate and evaluate millions of drug-like compounds continuously in parallel, slowing down lead identification and optimization phases in pharmaceutical pipelines. There is a critical need for architectures and data infrastructures that scale molecular generation and evaluation seamlessly with real-world biological feedback.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-agent reinforcement learning (RL) framework orchestrated on a distributed cloud platform where millions of candidate molecules are generated via Transformer-based molecular generators fine-tuned with policy gradient methods. The platform integrates real-time feedback from heterogeneous biological assays (binding affinity, toxicity, ADME properties) through streaming data pipelines using Apache Kafka and cloud-native databases (e.g., Google BigQuery). A scalable distributed training architecture employing Horovod for synchronous RL policy updates across GPU clusters enables efficient learning from continuous multi-assay signals. Molecular representations incorporate graph neural networks augmented with 3D conformer embeddings to enhance structural realism during generation. An adaptive reward shaping mechanism dynamically weights multi-objective biological endpoints to prioritize candidates with optimal therapeutic profiles.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on benchmark molecular generation datasets ZINC15, ChEMBL30, and the MoleculeNet (specifically Tox21 and HIV subsets). Baseline comparisons include ORGANIC, MolDQN, and REINVENT. Quantitatively measure:  
- Validity, uniqueness, and novelty of generated molecules (target >90% validity, >85% uniqueness)  
- Multi-objective optimization performance: achieve >5% absolute improvement in ROC-AUC on Tox21 toxicity prediction and >10% increase in docking score-based binding affinity against HIV protease versus REINVENT  
- Throughput scalability: demonstrate >10× speedup in molecules generated and evaluated per hour compared to baseline single-node RL methods  
Experimental protocol involves training on a Google Cloud TPU/GPU cluster budget of 5000 GPU-hours, running each experiment with 5 random seeds for statistical robustness, plus ablation studies on reward shaping and feedback integration latency.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that either focuses on single-target molecular generation or static offline datasets, our approach enables continuous, industrial-scale molecular generation tightly coupled with real-time, multi-assay biological feedback in a high-throughput cloud environment. This integrated platform architecture and adaptive multi-objective reinforcement learning framework have not been demonstrated before, directly addressing throughput and feedback integration bottlenecks that currently limit practical deployment in pharmaceutical lead discovery pipelines.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an evolutionary generative framework that rapidly adapts molecular designs to individual patient biochemical and genetic profiles by integrating meta-learning with constrained evolutionary search to achieve fast convergence and precise personalization from extremely limited patient data.

BACKGROUND:  
Current personalized drug discovery methods using generative AI face critical challenges in adapting molecular designs swiftly to individual patients with limited samples and diverse biochemical constraints. Existing approaches, including fine-tuned Transformer and evolutionary models, suffer from slow convergence and insufficient adaptability, preventing timely therapeutic design in clinical settings where rapid response to patient heterogeneity is crucial. This bottleneck hinders effective precision therapeutics, especially under tight data scarcity and urgent treatment timelines.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid meta-evolutionary generative model that combines meta-learning-based initialization of molecular generative networks with a novel constrained evolutionary algorithm incorporating patient-specific biochemical and genetic constraints as hard fitness penalties. The method employs a fast-adapting molecular variational autoencoder (VAE) pre-trained on multi-omics linked drug-target interaction datasets (e.g., Therapeutics Data Commons) and fine-tunes it via meta-gradient updates to quickly adapt to new patient data. Subsequently, an adaptive evolutionary search guided by constrained multi-objective optimization balances exploration of chemical space with exploitation of patient-specific targets, accelerating convergence within limited evaluation budgets. The framework explicitly encodes genetic variants and metabolic profiles to enforce biochemical feasibility and toxicity constraints dynamically during molecule generation.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on benchmark datasets including Therapeutics Data Commons (TDC), ChEMBL 29, and the Genomics of Drug Sensitivity in Cancer (GDSC) dataset, using baselines such as MolGPT, ChemVAE, and EvoMol. Metrics include >15% absolute improvement in molecular validity and uniqueness, >10% increase in predicted binding affinity specificity to patient-derived targets (via docking scores from AutoDock Vina), and reduction of adaptation time by >40% compared to baseline fine-tuning approaches. Experimental setup includes training on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of 10,000 GPU hours, performing 5 random seeds per setting, and ablations on the meta-learning component, constraint enforcement strategies, and evolutionary parameters.

NOVELTY:
