Unlike prior work that fine-tunes generative models or applies general evolutionary search without explicit patient-specific constraints or rapid adaptation mechanisms, this framework uniquely integrates meta-learning for initialization with a constrained evolutionary algorithm tailored to individual patient molecular profiles, enabling fast convergence and high fidelity personalization unattainable by existing Transformer- or GAN-based methods. This allows real-time, precision molecular design in clinically relevant timeframes from extremely limited patient data.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a neuro-symbolic generative AI platform that integrates dynamic, user-defined symbolic constraints with multi-objective molecular optimization and real-time interactive feedback for medicinal chemists, enabling precise, rule-guided drug candidate generation that simultaneously satisfies structural, pharmacological, and ADMET criteria.

BACKGROUND:  
Current drug discovery AI tools either generate molecules purely from learned distributions or incorporate static domain rules without interactive real-time updates, limiting medicinal chemists’ ability to enforce complex, evolving constraints during design. Additionally, existing systems rarely support multi-objective optimization (e.g., potency, toxicity, synthetic accessibility) under explicit user control, leaving a gap in interpretable, flexible, and clinically relevant molecule generation with compliance to medicinal chemistry heuristics.

APPROACH:  
We propose a neuro-symbolic molecular design platform combining a transformer-based generative model with an integrated symbolic reasoning engine that accepts medicinal chemist-specified constraints—including substructure presence/absence, physicochemical property ranges, and ADMET filters—updated dynamically through an intuitive interface. The system employs multi-objective reinforcement learning to optimize generated molecules against these constraints and known pharmacological profiles, continuously refining output molecules in real time according to user edits and domain rules. Constraint satisfaction is ensured via on-the-fly symbolic pruning and neural guidance, enabling immediate feedback loops for interactive molecular exploration.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on DrugBank, ChEMBL (version 30), and the QM9 molecular dataset with benchmarks comparing against: MolGPT, REINVENT 3.0, and GraphAF baselines. Metrics include constraint compliance rate (>90%), multi-objective optimization improvement (≥10% increase in combined drug-likeness score), and user satisfaction scores (>4 on a 5-point Likert scale) from medicinal chemist studies. Experiments will run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with five random seeds per setting and include ablation studies removing symbolic constraints or multi-objective components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, our method uniquely fuses neuro-symbolic generation with interactive, dynamic user constraints and multi-objective optimization in a single closed-loop system—enabling medicinal chemists to iteratively steer molecule generation in real time with guaranteed compliance to complex, evolving chemical rules, a capability absent in current generative drug design frameworks.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Integrate reinforcement learning with noise-robust multi-objective optimization and adaptive reward shaping into a modular, automated high-throughput lead optimization pipeline to efficiently prioritize drug candidates from ultra-large chemical libraries under noisy, conflicting evaluation conditions.

BACKGROUND:  
Current automated lead optimization workflows suffer from slow evaluation cycles and inability to robustly handle noisy, conflicting objectives at scale, limiting throughput and delaying candidate prioritization in drug discovery. Existing generative AI models generate promising molecules but lack integration with scalable, noise-tolerant optimization frameworks that can efficiently navigate multi-objective trade-offs across millions of candidates. Overcoming this bottleneck is critical for accelerating the transition from molecule generation to actionable drug leads.

APPROACH:  
We propose a reinforcement learning-based pipeline that leverages scalable, adaptive reward mechanisms designed to tolerate noisy assay data and conflicting optimization objectives such as potency, selectivity, toxicity, and synthetic accessibility. The method incorporates: (1) a multi-agent RL setup with ensemble critics to estimate robust value functions under noise, (2) a dynamic, reward shaping strategy that prioritizes objectives adaptively based on real-time uncertainty estimates, and (3) integration with fast in silico evaluation models (e.g., docking, ADMET predictors) to enable high-throughput screening. The pipeline is modular, supporting plug-and-play molecular generative models (e.g., graph-based VAEs, transformer-based generators) and can scale evaluation across millions of compounds on HPC clusters.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on benchmark datasets including ZINC15, ChEMBL (v29), and the Therapeutics Data Commons (TDC) Drug Response dataset. Baselines include state-of-the-art generative models and optimization pipelines such as REINVENT, MolDQN, and GraphAF. Metrics will measure multi-objective optimization success: achieving >5% absolute improvement in Pareto front hypervolume, a >20% reduction in false positive lead prioritization under simulated noise, and a >2x throughput speedup in candidate evaluation compared to REINVENT. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with at least 5 seeds per configuration, including ablations on reward shaping and ensemble critic components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that focuses primarily on generative model architectures or single-objective optimization, this research explicitly addresses noisy, multi-objective lead optimization at scale by integrating adaptive, noise-tolerant RL strategies into a high-throughput pipeline, enabling robust candidate prioritization across millions of molecules—a capability lacking in current generative AI drug discovery frameworks.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable physics-informed generative AI framework optimized for cloud HPC clusters that integrates differentiable molecular simulations with distributed training to enable ultra-high-throughput virtual screening of billions of drug-like molecules without sacrificing physical fidelity.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing generative AI frameworks for drug discovery either rely on purely data-driven molecular generation or physics-agnostic heuristics, limiting their ability to produce physically valid compounds at scale. Furthermore, physics-informed generative models incorporating simulations suffer from prohibitive computational costs and poor scalability on cloud HPC, preventing their application to ultra-large chemical spaces needed for efficient drug candidate identification. This bottleneck restricts the practical utility of generative AI in high-throughput virtual screening workflows.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid architecture combining differentiable physics-based molecular simulators (e.g., differentiable force fields or quantum chemistry approximations) with a novel distributed graph-based generative model optimized for cloud cluster environments. The model employs physics-constrained variational autoencoders augmented with multi-node parallelism and asynchronous gradient updates to scale training and inference across thousands of GPUs. A dynamic workload scheduler balances simulation fidelity and throughput, selectively invoking high-accuracy physics calculations only for promising candidates. This approach tightly couples generative design with scalable physics evaluation to efficiently explore ultra-large chemical libraries.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on benchmark datasets including ZINC15 (250M molecules), ChEMBL27, and the DUD-E dataset, comparing generation quality and screening throughput. Baselines include MolDQN, GraphAF, and physics-informed methods like PhysChemGAN. Metrics include valid molecule ratio (>95%), docking score improvement (>10% over baselines on DUD-E), wall-clock screening throughput (target >10M molecules/day on a 1024-GPU cloud cluster), and physics constraint violation rate (<2%). Ablation studies will measure effects of simulation fidelity thresholds and parallelism strategies. Experiments run with a fixed compute budget of ~10,000 GPU-hours on NVIDIA A100 or equivalent.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior generative AI models that either neglect physics fidelity or fail to scale on cloud HPC, this method uniquely integrates differentiable physics simulations within a distributed generative framework optimized for massive parallelism. It addresses the critical gap of balancing physical accuracy and computational efficiency at extreme scale, enabling rapid virtual screening of ultra-large chemical spaces unattainable by existing approaches.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
A resource- and communication-efficient meta-federated learning framework that integrates sparse adaptive personalization layers with robust noise-aware model aggregation to enable fast, privacy-preserving on-device adaptation on heterogeneous health monitoring devices with sparse, noisy, and highly non-IID data.

BACKGROUND:  
Personal health monitoring devices produce sparse, noisy, and highly non-IID sensor data across users with strict constraints on compute, memory, and communication bandwidth. Existing federated meta-learning methods either fail to adapt quickly with minimal local data or require heavy on-device computation and communication, inhibiting personalized, privacy-preserving health insights. Moreover, current solutions inadequately address simultaneous heterogeneity in data distributions, device capabilities, and noise characteristics, leaving a critical gap for real-world personalized health monitoring deployment.

APPROACH:  
We propose MetaSparseFed, a federated meta-learning framework that (1) decouples global model parameters into a frozen backbone and lightweight adaptive sparse personalization layers that update rapidly with minimal data and compute on-device, (2) incorporates a noise-robust adaptive weighting scheme during server aggregation that accounts for client-specific noise levels and heterogeneous data distributions to mitigate client drift, and (3) leverages communication-efficient model compression and asynchronous update scheduling personalized per device capability to reduce communication overhead. This combination enables fast, accurate on-device personalization while preserving privacy and respecting device heterogeneity and noisy non-IID data.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark health datasets: PAMAP2 Physical Activity Monitoring, MIMIC-III Clinical Database (for wearable vitals), and WESAD Stress and Affect Detection dataset. Baselines include FedGKD (2021), HeteroSFL (2025), and Adaptive Dual-Mode Distillation (2025). Metrics include >3% absolute improvement on F1 score for personalized activity/stress detection, reduction of communication overhead by ≥40%, and on-device adaptation latency below 500ms on simulated resource-constrained hardware. Experiments run with 5 random seeds, ablations on sparse layer size, noise-aware weighting, and asynchronous updates, under a training compute budget of 48 GPU-hours distributed across edge-device simulators and a central server.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that addresses either heterogeneity or communication/resource constraints separately, MetaSparseFed jointly optimizes sparse personalization, noise-robust aggregation, and adaptive communication tailored to heterogeneous, noisy, sparse health data and device limitations, enabling precise, fast on-device adaptation that prior federated meta-learning methods fail to achieve simultaneously.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a dynamic, memory- and communication-aware hierarchical client clustering framework that integrates resource-constrained graph pruning with adaptive local-global knowledge distillation to optimize federated learning on heterogeneous clients with highly non-IID data.

BACKGROUND:  
Current federated learning approaches struggle to scale effectively on heterogeneous clients due to memory and computation limitations, static or dense client collaboration graphs causing resource overload, and poor handling of severe data distribution skew. Existing graph-based clustering methods, including the winning Memory-Constrained Graph-Based Client Clustering with Resource-Aware Edge Pruning, reduce memory but do not jointly optimize communication overhead nor fully address client-drift caused by non-IID data. Moreover, prior work on knowledge distillation techniques like FedGKD and dual-mode distillation improve accuracy but typically assume fixed client sets and homogeneous resource availability, limiting adaptability in dynamic real-world edge environments.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical federated learning framework that first constructs a sparse, resource-aware client collaboration graph via dynamic edge pruning considering both client memory and communication bandwidth constraints. On this graph, clients are clustered into localized groups for semi-decentralized model aggregation, reducing client memory and communication bottlenecks. Within each cluster, we employ adaptive local-global knowledge distillation where local models distill knowledge from both cluster-level aggregated models and historical global models to mitigate client-drift from non-IID data. Additionally, the framework dynamically adjusts clustering topology and distillation weights in response to client resource fluctuations, enabling continuous scalability and robustness under heterogeneous and evolving device conditions.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate the framework on Stack Overflow, CelebA, and CIFAR-100 federated datasets to capture diverse non-IID data distributions and modalities. Baselines include Memory-Constrained Graph-Based Client Clustering with Resource-Aware Edge Pruning (the tournament winner), FedGKD, and Adaptive Dual-Mode Distillation. Metrics measured will be test accuracy (targeting >2% absolute improvement over baselines), per-client memory consumption (aiming for ≥25% reduction), communication overhead measured in MB transmitted per round (targeting ≥30% reduction), and convergence speed (expecting ≥20% fewer rounds to target accuracy). Experiments will be run on a simulated heterogeneous edge environment with varying client memory (100MB-1GB RAM) and network bandwidth (100Kbps-10Mbps), with 5 random seeds and ablation studies on edge pruning thresholds and distillation weighting.

NOVELTY:  
This approach uniquely integrates memory- and communication-aware dynamic graph pruning with hierarchical clustering and adaptive local-global knowledge distillation, addressing both resource constraints and client-drift simultaneously. Unlike prior work, it adapts collaboration topology and distillation parameters in real-time to heterogeneous client resource fluctuations, enabling scalable and accurate federated learning in truly dynamic, heterogeneous edge environments with highly non-IID data.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Design a meta-learned federated personalization framework that leverages client-adaptive split learning with dynamic communication scheduling to efficiently train natural language understanding (NLU) models on heterogeneous smartphones with non-IID data, minimizing communication overhead while enabling fast user adaptation under strict resource constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Federated learning for heterogeneous clients with non-IID language data on smartphones faces a critical trade-off between personalization speed, communication cost, and computational load. Existing meta-learning methods for personalization either assume homogeneous data or impose heavy communication/computation overheads, making them impractical for resource-constrained devices with diverse linguistic patterns. Meanwhile, split federated learning reduces client-side compute but suffers from high communication costs and lacks mechanisms for rapid personalization tailored to each user’s unique data distribution.

APPROACH:  
We propose a meta-learning framework integrating split federated learning with dynamic client-specific communication scheduling and lightweight local adaptation modules. The global model is partitioned between server and clients via split learning layers optimized for minimal client compute. A meta-learner trains initialization parameters and adaptation hyperparameters specifically tuned for rapid fine-tuning on each client’s non-IID data with few local gradient steps. Communication rounds are adaptively scheduled per client based on device resource availability and data heterogeneity, employing a learned policy that prioritizes clients with higher personalization gain potential. The approach also incorporates local-global knowledge distillation to stabilize training and reduce client drift.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on real federated NLU benchmarks, including GLUE tasks (e.g., SST-2, MRPC), CLINC150 for intent classification, and the Federated Stack Overflow dataset for language modeling. Compare against baselines: standard FedAvg, HeteroSFL, FedGKD, and the winning meta-learning method “Meta-Learning for Fast Personalization of NLU on Smartphones.” Metrics include task accuracy/F1 score (target >2% absolute improvement over baselines on GLUE tasks), communication cost (target >30% reduction in rounds/data transmitted), and local adaptation latency (target <50ms on simulated resource-constrained smartphones). Experiments run on GPU/server clusters simulating 100 heterogeneous clients over 5 seeds, with ablations on communication scheduling and split layer depth.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior SFL methods that focus only on homogeneous clients or static communication, and meta-learning works that neglect communication efficiency, this method uniquely combines meta-learned fast personalization with communication-adaptive split federated learning tailored to heterogeneous, non-IID smartphone data, achieving practical deployment feasibility with provable resource savings and personalization speedups.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a Federated Learning framework that dynamically learns and updates a heterogeneous client collaboration graph by integrating temporal device behavior embedding and uncertainty-aware edge pruning to robustly handle heterogeneous and non-IID data distributions under rapid domain shifts.

BACKGROUND:  
Current federated learning methods with static or periodically updated client collaboration graphs struggle to adapt to the rapid and heterogeneous domain shifts typical in industrial IoT and edge environments, causing stale or irrelevant client collaborations that degrade model accuracy. Furthermore, existing SFL and decentralized FL approaches inadequately address heterogeneity in device capabilities and data distributions while incurring high communication cost or experiencing convergence delays. There is an open problem in designing a communication-efficient, real-time adaptive collaboration mechanism that captures temporal client behavior and heterogeneity to improve learning from non-IID data.

APPROACH:  
We propose a Dynamic Heterogeneous Graph Federated Learning (DHG-FL) framework that embeds client sensory data streams into temporal latent representations via lightweight recurrent encoders deployed locally. These embeddings enable continuous computation of similarity scores weighted by uncertainty estimates derived from client model confidence. Edges in the client collaboration graph are dynamically updated through an uncertainty-aware pruning and addition mechanism to preserve only statistically meaningful collaborations, reducing noise from distribution shifts and device heterogeneity. The graph topology guides federated aggregation via a weighted graph convolutional aggregator that adapts to evolving client distributions. The framework incorporates split learning principles to offload heavy computation to servers, mitigating client resource constraints and communication overhead.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate DHG-FL on SECOM and PHM08 manufacturing datasets alongside the Human Activity Recognition (HAR) dataset to capture diverse sensor modalities and heterogeneity patterns. Baselines include Dynamic Graph Structure Learning (DGSL), HeteroSFL, FedGKD, and Adaptive Dual-Mode Distillation. Metrics to assess include predictive maintenance accuracy (target >3% absolute improvement over DGSL), communication latency (target <25% reduction in graph update overhead), and convergence speed (target 20% fewer communication rounds). The experimental protocol involves training on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with federated simulations across 30 clients with heterogeneous compute profiles, running 5 random seeds per configuration, and ablation studies on embedding dimension, pruning threshold, and aggregation strategy.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike DGSL which lacks uncertainty modeling and edge pruning, and HeteroSFL which does not leverage dynamic collaboration graphs, DHG-FL explicitly fuses temporal client embedding with uncertainty-aware graph topology adaptation to robustly and efficiently address rapid non-IID data shifts and client heterogeneity simultaneously. This integration enables real-time, noise-resilient client collaboration that prior methods cannot achieve without sacrificing communication efficiency or accuracy.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA: Develop a privacy-compliant, regulation-aware meta-learning framework for cross-silo federated learning that explicitly incorporates heterogeneous client constraints and non-IID data distributions through adaptive, compliance-driven personalization and privacy budget allocation.

BACKGROUND: Cross-silo federated learning scenarios involving enterprise servers with large, heterogeneous, and non-IID datasets face the challenge of balancing high personalization accuracy with strict privacy and regulatory compliance requirements (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA). Existing meta-learning or federated learning methods either neglect compliance constraints or fail to optimize personalization under heterogeneous data and privacy budgets, limiting model utility and practical deployment in regulated environments.

APPROACH: We propose “Compliance-Aware Meta-Federated Learning” (CAMFL), a two-level meta-learning algorithm that integrates differential privacy with adaptive privacy budget allocation per client based on their regulatory and data sensitivity profiles. CAMFL incorporates a compliance constraint module that dynamically modulates model adaptation steps and local update granularity to respect client-specific governance rules while maximizing model utility. Additionally, CAMFL uses a personalized meta-gradient aggregation scheme that weights client contributions by compliance risk and data distribution heterogeneity to mitigate non-IID induced model drift. Privacy-preserving techniques such as Rényi Differential Privacy accounting and secure aggregation protocols are embedded to enforce provable privacy guarantees during meta-training.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate CAMFL on large-scale, heterogeneous cross-silo datasets including MIMIC-III (clinical time-series), Fed-EMNIST (handwritten character recognition across users), and the Large Movie Review dataset (IMDB sentiment classification). Baselines include FedAvg, FedMeta, and Privacy-Compliant Meta-Learning (the tournament winner method). Metrics will be (i) test accuracy or AUC (targeting >3% absolute improvement over baselines), (ii) privacy budget consumption (ε in (ε, δ)-DP, aiming for 30% reduction in ε for equivalent accuracy), and (iii) compliance violation rate measured by custom constraint satisfaction metrics. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 seeds per configuration, including ablations on privacy budget allocation and compliance constraint modules.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that treats privacy and compliance as fixed constraints or does not adapt meta-learning to client heterogeneity in regulatory context, CAMFL dynamically integrates regulatory compliance into the meta-learning loop, enabling principled trade-offs between personalization performance and privacy governance in large-scale, diverse cross-silo environments with non-IID data. It is the first to unify compliance-aware adaptation, privacy budget optimization, and heterogeneity-aware meta-aggregation under one federated meta-learning framework.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable Bayesian deep learning emulator framework that integrates hierarchical latent process modeling with temporal nonstationarity adaptation and structural error correction for robust long-term climate risk quantification over multi-decadal horizons.

BACKGROUND:  
Current deep learning climate emulators inadequately quantify uncertainty over long simulation periods, primarily due to inability to model evolving nonstationarity and structural errors in climate system dynamics. This limits their reliability for multi-decadal risk assessment and decision support under climate change. The open problem is to design a computationally efficient emulator that provides calibrated, scalable uncertainty estimates reflecting both internal variability and evolving climate model biases across decades.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical Bayesian deep recurrent architecture combining latent Gaussian processes with attention-based temporal adaptation modules to explicitly model climate process nonstationarity and structural errors across time. The emulator jointly learns predictive mean and heteroscedastic uncertainty by leveraging variational inference with stochastic gradient MCMC, enabling scalable posterior approximation over long horizons. Structural error correction layers parameterize residual errors conditioned on learned climate state embeddings, ensuring improved calibration. The framework is trained end-to-end on multi-model ensembles and observational benchmarks for robust uncertainty quantification and transferability.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on CMIP6 multi-model ensemble outputs (e.g., CMIP6 Historical + SSP5-8.5 scenario data), the CORDEX regional climate projection datasets, and the ClimateBench benchmark dataset for climate emulator evaluation. Baselines include DeepSD, Bayesian U-Net, and DeepAR emulators. Metrics are continuous ranked probability score (CRPS) with >10% reduction over baselines, calibration error <5%, and skillful prediction of decadal trends with RMSE improvement >15%. Training conducted on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 4 seeds, ablation studies on latent process components and structural error modules.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike existing emulators which either lack scalable uncertainty quantification (DeepSD) or do not explicitly model temporal nonstationarity and structural errors (Bayesian U-Net, DeepAR), our method jointly addresses evolving climate dynamics and structural bias with a unified hierarchical Bayesian temporal modeling approach. This enables credible, computationally feasible uncertainty quantification for long-term climate risk assessment beyond current SOTA capabilities.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a memory- and compute-efficient physics-informed neural network (PINN) architecture with innovative physical law encoding and lightweight training routines for real-time, edge-deployable climate forecast emulation that balances accuracy, latency, and energy consumption.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning emulators for climate models lack the ability to embed physical constraints within lightweight architectures suitable for edge devices, limiting their applicability to real-time, decentralized forecasting in resource-constrained environments. This gap hinders timely, localized climate predictions critical for vulnerable regions requiring immediate action. Existing PINN approaches are generally too heavy or lack optimized training for hardware-limited, low-latency inference.

APPROACH: We propose a novel PINN architecture that integrates sparse physical law encodings—such as discretized mass and energy conservation constraints—directly into a compressed neural network backbone tailored for low-memory edge hardware. The method includes an adaptive training scheme combining multi-objective loss balancing physical consistency with predictive accuracy and a hardware-aware pruning and quantization pipeline. This approach enables deployment on embedded platforms with strict compute and energy budgets, facilitating scalable, real-time climate emulation.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the WeatherBench (2018) dataset for midlatitude weather forecasting, the ERA5 reanalysis dataset for climate surface variables, and the CORDEX regional climate projections dataset. Baselines include the original Physics-Informed Neural Networks (Raissi et al., 2019), DeepSD (Vandal et al., 2017), and the U-Net uncertainty-aware ozone emulator (2025). Metrics will track >5% improvement in physical consistency scores (e.g., conservation error reduction), latency reductions of ≥40% on NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX edge devices, and energy consumption drops of ≥30%, while maintaining RMSE within 2% of baseline accuracy. Protocol involves training on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a fixed 72-hour compute budget, deploying on Jetson hardware, and conducting ablations on pruning levels, loss weighting, and physical constraint formulations, with five random seeds per experiment for statistical significance.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior climate emulators that focus on accuracy or downscaling without embedding physics or optimizing for edge deployment, this work uniquely integrates physics-informed constraints into a compact, hardware-aware architecture with a novel multi-objective training regime, enabling real-time, physically consistent climate forecasts on resource-limited devices—a capability absent in existing methods.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a probabilistic deep learning climate emulator architecture that delivers real-time, low-latency uncertainty quantification robust to sensor noise and distribution shifts, enabling trustworthy adaptive climate control decisions under dynamic environmental conditions.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning climate emulators do not provide sufficiently fast or robust uncertainty estimates for real-time adaptive climate control, where delayed or unreliable uncertainty quantification impedes safe decision-making. Existing methods either sacrifice inference speed for uncertainty fidelity or deteriorate under sensor noise and domain shifts, limiting their operational viability in dynamic, data-driven climate control systems.

APPROACH: We propose a hybrid Bayesian deep learning emulator combining deep ensemble methods with a lightweight, uncertainty-aware transformer backbone optimized for spatiotemporal climate data. This model integrates heteroscedastic aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty via variational dropout and deep ensembles, with an adaptive calibration module that dynamically adjusts uncertainty outputs under distribution shifts using online sensor feedback. The architecture is designed for sub-100ms inference on edge GPUs to support real-time control loops.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the WeatherBench dataset (2018), the ERA5 reanalysis data, and the CMIP6 multi-model ensemble downscaled outputs, benchmarking against Deep Ensemble U-Net, Bayesian U-Net, and quantile regression baselines. Metrics include predictive RMSE (target <10% improvement), negative log-likelihood (NLL) reduction >0.1, calibration error (Expected Calibration Error, ECE <5%), and inference latency (target <100ms per sample). Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 training seeds per configuration, including ablations on uncertainty components and the adaptive calibration module.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior deep learning emulators which either lack robust uncertainty quantification or cannot operate at low latency, this method uniquely combines scalable Bayesian uncertainty approximations with an adaptive calibration mechanism tailored for real-time, noise-robust climate control. This enables operational deployment where trustworthy, low-latency uncertainty estimates directly inform adaptive climate policies under non-stationary conditions.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a scalable physics-aware Bayesian deep learning emulator that jointly models aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty for large-scale climate ensembles, enabling robust uncertainty quantification for regional climate projections and risk assessment.

BACKGROUND: Existing deep learning climate emulators either omit uncertainty quantification or provide limited, non-scalable uncertainty estimates that fail to capture both data noise and model structural uncertainties across large ensembles. This shortcoming restricts their applicability in policy-relevant risk assessments requiring reliable probabilistic forecasts over multiple scenarios and spatial scales. The open problem is to create an emulator framework that can provide scalable, high-fidelity uncertainty quantification integrating physical constraints and ensemble diversity.

APPROACH: We propose a novel hybrid emulator combining a physics-informed neural network backbone with a scalable Bayesian approximation method (e.g., variational inference with Deep Ensembles or Bayesian last-layer inference) that explicitly models both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. The model will be trained on multi-model, multi-scenario regional climate simulation datasets, embedding physical consistency via differentiable constraints on energy balance and moisture conservation. The approach leverages multi-task learning to predict multiple climate variables jointly, improving uncertainty calibration and robustness to internal variability.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on CORDEX regional climate model datasets (e.g., EURO-CORDEX, AFRICA-CORDEX, and NA-CORDEX) alongside the CMIP6 large ensemble datasets (e.g., CESM2 LE and MPI-ESM1.2-LR Large Ensemble). Baselines include the Deep Ensemble emulator, Bayesian U-Net from “Uncertainty Quantification for Surface Ozone Emulators,” and the deep learning RCM downscaling framework from 2023. Metrics include Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) (targeting >10% reduction vs baselines), calibration error (expected calibration error, ECE, below 5%), and spatial-temporal pattern correlation (improving by >0.05 over baselines). Experiments will run on multi-GPU clusters with 3 independent random seeds and ablation on physics constraints and uncertainty modeling components.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work, this approach simultaneously scales to large multi-model, multi-scenario climate ensembles with integrated physics-informed constraints and a principled Bayesian treatment of uncertainty, overcoming the limited uncertainty quantification and poor ensemble scalability in existing emulators. It explicitly models and disentangles aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty, enabling risk assessments with robust probabilistic forecasts unavailable in previous deep learning climate emulator frameworks.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Introduce a scalable sparse expert coordination framework with dynamic multivariable gating to efficiently emulate global climate models by capturing complex inter-variable dependencies while eliminating expert redundancy and enhancing interpretability.

BACKGROUND: Current global climate emulators struggle to accurately model the nonlinear interactions among multiple climate variables simultaneously without incurring expert redundancy or sacrificing interpretability. Existing methods either oversimplify multivariable dependencies or activate overlapping experts, leading to inefficiencies and opaque model behavior. This limits the ability to generate comprehensive, interpretable global climate projections that reflect key coupled processes at scale.

APPROACH: We propose a novel deep learning architecture that integrates a sparse gating mechanism dynamically conditioned on multivariable climate states to selectively activate a coordinated ensemble of expert subnetworks, each specialized in particular variable interactions. Coordination among experts is enforced through a learned orthogonality regularization and cross-expert attention modules to prevent redundancy and encourage complementary representation learning. An interpretable gating mask visualization combined with expert attribution analysis enables transparent understanding of the multivariate climate dynamics captured. The model is trained end-to-end using a multitask loss over multiple climate variables to ensure coherent joint emulation.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluation will be performed on the CMIP6 multi-model ensemble dataset (focusing on historical and SSP scenarios), the NASA MERRA-2 reanalysis dataset, and the PRISM climate dataset for regional validation. Baselines include the Sparse Mixture of Experts model (Shazeer et al., 2017), the Global Climate Emulation with Multivariable Interactions via Sparse Expert Coordination (the tournament winner), and the Deep Learning Regional Climate Model Emulator from Vandal et al. (2023). Metrics will include normalized root mean squared error (NRMSE) improvement >3% over baselines on temperature and precipitation jointly, a reduction in active expert count per inference by ≥25%, and interpretability measured by gate sparsity and expert attribution consistency scores. Experimental protocol involves training on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a fixed compute budget of 100 GPU hours per run, averaging results over 5 random seeds, and ablation studies testing the gating sparsity regularizer and coordination modules separately.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work, this approach introduces explicit expert coordination via orthogonality and cross-attention to systematically eliminate expert redundancy while enabling interpretable sparse gating conditioned on multivariable states. Prior models either oversimplify interactions or activate redundant experts without interpretability controls, limiting scalability and transparency. This method enables accurate, efficient, and interpretable global emulation of multivariate climate dynamics at scale that was previously unachieved.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Integrate instrumental variable-based Bayesian causal inference into deep neural networks’ latent space to quantify and calibrate uncertainty arising specifically from unmeasured confounding and data sparsity in observational public health datasets.

BACKGROUND:  
Current deep neural network uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods fail to explicitly account for unmeasured confounding in observational data, leading to misleadingly narrow uncertainty intervals and biased effect estimates in policy evaluation tasks, especially in public health. Existing UQ approaches (e.g., Bayesian neural nets, ensembles) do not separate uncertainty due to confounding from other sources, reducing the reliability of uncertainty estimates under data sparsity and confounding. This gap critically undermines decisions based on causal effect estimates in public health interventions.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel framework—Causal Instrumental Variable Uncertainty Quantification Network (CIV-UQNet)—that embeds instrumental variable (IV) estimation within a Bayesian deep latent-variable model. The method jointly learns a latent representation of observational data and infers posterior distributions over policy effect estimates by leveraging IV assumptions to isolate causal pathways, explicitly modeling uncertainty arising from unmeasured confounders. The network incorporates a probabilistic encoder-decoder architecture conditioned on IVs, augmented with a hierarchical Bayesian layer that propagates confounding-induced uncertainty through the latent space. Posterior inference is performed via variational approximations calibrated by importance-weighted evidence lower bounds, ensuring tight uncertainty intervals sensitive to confounding strength and sample size.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate CIV-UQNet on COVID-19 intervention datasets such as the COVID-19 Mobility Data Network, the COVID-19 US State Policy Database, and the IHME COVID-19 Forecasting dataset, where known confounders and instrumental variables have been identified. Baselines will include Bayesian Neural Networks (BNN), Monte Carlo Dropout (MCD), Deep Ensembles, and the Probabilistic Skip Connections model (PSC). Metrics include: average calibration error (ACE) of uncertainty intervals (targeting >10% reduction vs baselines), coverage probability of 95% posterior credible intervals (targeting >95% coverage), and bias reduction in causal effect estimates (>15% reduction in mean squared error). Experiments will use a compute budget of 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, training with 5 random seeds, including ablations comparing latent space IV integration vs vanilla UQ methods.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior UQ methods that treat uncertainty as a monolithic quantity, CIV-UQNet explicitly disentangles and quantifies uncertainty due to unmeasured confounding by integrating instrumental variable causal inference directly within deep latent representations. This addresses a critical deficiency in existing work—such as probabilistic skip connections and active subspace methods—that assume confounding-free settings and cannot reliably quantify policy effect uncertainty under confounding and sparse data.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a meta-learned hierarchical Bayesian fusion framework that dynamically adapts uncertainty quantification of heterogeneous sensor streams by modeling domain shifts as latent environment variables, enabling robust uncertainty calibration and sensor reliability estimation under rapid non-stationary conditions in manufacturing.

BACKGROUND:  
Current uncertainty quantification methods for multi-sensor fusion, including recent meta-learned Bayesian fusion modules, struggle to maintain well-calibrated uncertainty estimates under rapid domain shifts caused by changing factory floor environments and sensor failures. Existing approaches, such as fixed-weight Bayesian fusion or single-level meta-learning, do not explicitly model the latent environmental factors driving non-stationarity or leverage hierarchical uncertainty decomposition, limiting robustness and reliability in real-world deployment.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical Bayesian fusion model with two levels of meta-learning: the first learns sensor-level uncertainty calibration and fusion weights conditioned on latent environmental context variables inferred online; the second learns to adapt these latent context variables from heterogeneous sensor data streams (vision, tactile, force). By explicitly modeling the environment as a latent variable via amortized variational inference, the fusion module dynamically recalibrates uncertainty and reweights sensors in response to non-stationarity. The framework also incorporates sensor failure detection via Bayesian reliability scores integrated into the fusion process. Training uses episodic meta-learning on factory floor data to optimize both fusion and latent context inference jointly.

EXPERIMENT:  
Validate on MIT Factory Floor Dataset, TUM Kitchen Dataset (multi-sensor robotics), and the KAIST Multi-Sensor Dataset, comparing against baselines including fixed-weight Bayesian fusion, the original meta-learned Bayesian fusion (winner method), and ensemble-based UQ methods like MC Dropout and Deep Ensembles. Quantitatively evaluate uncertainty calibration (Expected Calibration Error, Negative Log-Likelihood), task accuracy (completion rate of robotic manipulation), and robustness to sensor failure via sensor dropout experiments, aiming for >20% reduction in Expected Calibration Error and >10% improvement in task completion accuracy over baselines. Conduct ablation studies on latent context modeling and hierarchical meta-learning components. Training budget assumes 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs for 72 hours, with 5 random seeds per experiment for statistical significance.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, this method explicitly models non-stationary domain shifts as latent environmental variables within a hierarchical Bayesian fusion framework, enabling dynamic adjustment of uncertainty calibration and sensor fusion weights in real time. It integrates sensor reliability estimation directly into the fusion process and uses two-level meta-learning to generalize uncertainty quantification across unseen factory floor conditions, a capability absent in fixed or single-level meta-learned fusion approaches.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a compact meta-learned uncertainty calibration framework with privacy-preserving efficient adaptation algorithms that deliver real-time, personalized uncertainty estimates on resource-constrained mobile health devices, robust to heterogeneous patient data distributions.

BACKGROUND:  
Personalized mobile health monitoring demands uncertainty quantification models that adapt quickly and reliably to individual patient data while operating under strict resource, privacy, and data heterogeneity constraints. Existing meta-learning-based uncertainty calibration methods are computationally intensive and fail to maintain accuracy when adapting to diverse patient distributions on-device, especially under privacy constraints limiting data sharing and model update frequency.

APPROACH:  
We propose a lightweight meta-learned calibration model based on a compact parameterization of uncertainty estimators using a low-rank decomposition of calibration layers combined with active subspace projections of model parameters to reduce adaptation complexity. Privacy-preserving meta-updates leverage federated learning with differential privacy constraints, while efficient adaptation employs a novel gradient projection technique that rapidly personalizes calibration with minimal compute. The method uses probabilistic skip connections (PSCs) to identify informative intermediate representations for uncertainty estimates, enhancing robustness to distribution shifts without requiring full model retraining.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three real-world mobile health datasets: MIMIC-III (clinical ICU data), WESAD (wearable stress and affect data), and PAMAP2 (physical activity monitoring). Baselines include existing meta-learned calibration approaches (e.g., Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning for Calibration), deep ensemble methods, and Monte Carlo Dropout. Metrics include Expected Calibration Error (ECE, targeting <5% absolute improvement over baselines), Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL, aiming for >10% reduction), and on-device latency and adaptation memory footprint (targeting at least 30% reduction compared to baselines). Experiments use a limited compute budget consistent with mobile CPU/GPU constraints, running 5 random seeds and full ablation studies on adaptation algorithms and privacy parameters.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, this approach uniquely combines compact meta-learned calibration with privacy-preserving federated meta-updates and efficient gradient projection for real-time personalization on mobile devices. It explicitly addresses heterogeneous, privacy-sensitive patient data adaptation with significantly reduced compute and memory overhead, a capability absent in current meta-learning calibration or uncertainty quantification methods.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a Robust Latent Space Density-Based Uncertainty Quantification framework that explicitly integrates semi-supervised latent density modeling with heteroscedastic feature disentanglement to accurately detect out-of-distribution and atypical cases in medical imaging under severely limited labeled data conditions.

BACKGROUND:  
Current latent space density estimation methods for uncertainty quantification (UQ) in medical imaging fail to reliably flag clinically critical atypical or out-of-distribution cases due to the scarcity of labeled data and the heterogeneous, high-dimensional nature of medical images. Existing techniques inadequately handle label scarcity and complexity simultaneously, leading to brittle uncertainty metrics that limit clinical applicability. The open problem is how to design an uncertainty metric that remains robust and clinically meaningful when labels are limited and data complexity is high.

APPROACH:  
We propose a unified framework combining (1) semi-supervised latent density estimation leveraging both labeled and unlabeled data via a variational autoencoder with a disentangled latent space explicitly separating heteroscedastic factors, and (2) a novel uncertainty metric computed from robust density estimations in this disentangled latent space that reflect both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties. This approach employs a label-conditional latent prior that adapts to limited supervision, uses a heteroscedastic disentanglement loss to separate noise from signal dimensions, and integrates a robust kernel density estimator tailored for high-dimensional latent embeddings of medical images. The uncertainty metric flags out-of-distribution and atypical samples by quantifying deviations in latent density and heteroscedastic variances jointly.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark medical imaging datasets with limited labels: CheXpert (thoracic radiographs), BraTS (brain tumor MRI), and ISIC 2018 (skin lesion images). Compare against baselines: Probabilistic Skip Connections (PSC, 2025), Uncertainty Quantification through Statistical Inference on Latent Space (2023), and Monte Carlo Dropout on ResNet-50. Metrics include AUROC and AUPR for out-of-distribution detection (targeting >5% absolute improvement over baselines), Expected Calibration Error (ECE) reduction by ≥3%, and false negative rate reduction on clinically relevant anomalies by >10%. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 3 random seeds per setting and will include ablations on latent disentanglement and semi-supervised label utilization.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike PSC and prior latent space inference methods that rely on fully supervised latent density estimation or single-mode uncertainty measures, our method explicitly models heteroscedastic factors and incorporates unlabeled data to robustly estimate uncertainty under label scarcity and heterogeneity. This dual disentanglement and semi-supervised density estimation in latent space enables meaningful uncertainty quantification that prior methods cannot achieve in limited labeled medical imaging scenarios.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable variational mutual information framework that jointly models temporal dependencies and multi-modal data fusion within deep neural networks to enable accurate, batch-efficient uncertainty quantification and risk forecasting in high-dimensional financial datasets.

BACKGROUND:  
Current uncertainty quantification methods in deep neural networks largely fail to scale to large batch sizes involving heterogeneous, multi-modal financial data (e.g., price, volume, sentiment), resulting in poor temporal consistency and inaccurate risk estimates. Existing approaches either ignore temporal dependencies in mutual information estimation or lack the computational efficiency to handle large-scale multi-modal inputs, limiting their applicability for real-time financial risk forecasting.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel variational mutual information estimation method integrating temporal dependency modeling via a structured latent variable framework that captures temporal coherence across financial time series and sentiment data. This approach employs a scalable variational inference algorithm optimized for large batches containing heterogeneous modalities, leveraging amortized inference with sequence-aware encoders (e.g., Transformer or TCN-based) to efficiently estimate mutual information. The framework outputs temporally calibrated uncertainty estimates that improve downstream financial risk prediction tasks, such as Value at Risk (VaR) and Expected Shortfall (ES).

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on: (1) LOBSTER (Limit Order Book data), (2) S&P 500 historical price + Twitter sentiment dataset, and (3) FI-2010 (Financial Instrument order book dataset). Baselines include: (a) Monte Carlo Dropout with LSTM, (b) Probabilistic Skip Connections (PSC) method (2025), and (c) Active Subspace UQ (2023). Metrics: mutual information estimation accuracy (measured by lower bound tightness and bias), temporal calibration of uncertainty (Temporal NLL and Expected Calibration Error over time), and financial risk forecasting improvements (>5% reduction in VaR backtesting exceptions and >3% improved ES estimation accuracy). Training will be conducted on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of up to 1000 GPU hours, using 5 random seeds per experiment and ablation on temporal vs non-temporal MI modeling and modality fusion strategies.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works that either do not model temporal dependencies within variational mutual information estimation or are limited to unimodal inputs and small batch sizes, our method jointly captures temporal dynamics and multi-modal correlations at scale with amortized variational inference, enabling temporally consistent uncertainty quantification that significantly improves financial risk metric reliability and scalability to industrial-scale financial datasets.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a structured pruning and knowledge distillation pipeline for compressing large masked genomic transformers into memory- and compute-efficient models that enable rapid, on-device few-shot fine-tuning for patient-specific single-cell omics classification on edge clinical devices.

BACKGROUND:  
Large pretrained masked genomic transformers capture rich biological context but are prohibitively large and slow to fine-tune on edge devices with limited memory and compute, preventing personalized single-cell omics diagnostics in clinical point-of-care settings. Existing compression or adaptation methods either sacrifice embedding quality or require extensive fine-tuning data, which is unavailable in clinical few-shot scenarios. This hinders rapid on-device model personalization critical for precision medicine.

APPROACH:  
We propose a compression framework combining structured pruning guided by biologically informed attention-head importance with knowledge distillation from the full-size masked genomic transformer to a compact student model optimized for few-shot adaptation. The student model retains critical contextual embeddings and leverages a lightweight adapter tuning module to enable rapid fine-tuning on ≤10 patient-specific labeled single-cell samples directly on edge devices. The pipeline includes iterative pruning-distillation cycles with a sparsity-aware loss and biologically constrained pruning masks focusing on genomic regulatory regions.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark single-cell genomics datasets: the Human Cell Atlas (HCA) single-cell RNA-seq, Tabula Sapiens, and the Kidney Precision Medicine Project (KPMP) single-cell kidney atlas. Baselines include the original uncompressed masked genomic transformer (MGformer), LoRA fine-tuning methods, and DistilBERT-style genomic model distillation. Metrics: achieve >70% model size reduction, >2% absolute accuracy gain in 10-shot classification tasks on patient-stratified splits, and reduce on-device fine-tuning latency by >50% on an ARM Cortex-A78 CPU. Protocol: 5 random seeds, ablations on pruning ratio and adapter size, training compute capped at 200 GPU hours on Nvidia A100s.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike existing single-cell foundation models and generic compression approaches, this work specifically targets few-shot patient-specific adaptation on edge devices by integrating biologically guided structured pruning with knowledge distillation optimized for minimal data regimes. Prior models do not achieve this balance of compression, rapid on-device fine-tuning, and accuracy retention in clinically relevant single-cell omics settings.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a scalable, cloud-native contrastive learning framework with optimized cross-dataset negative sampling and domain-adaptive batch correction to integrate multi-modal, multi-institutional single-cell atlases with high biological fidelity and computational efficiency.

BACKGROUND: Current single-cell foundation models fail to effectively integrate very large, heterogeneous single-cell datasets distributed across institutions and modalities due to batch effects, domain shifts, and computational bottlenecks in leveraging parallel cloud resources. This limits the construction of comprehensive, high-resolution single-cell atlases necessary for robust biological insights and reproducibility across cohorts.

APPROACH: We propose Large-scale Cloud-based Single-cell Atlas Integration (LCSAI), a method that combines (1) distributed contrastive learning with cross-dataset negative sampling to robustly learn shared latent representations, (2) domain-adaptive batch effect correction layers trained end-to-end to preserve biological signals, and (3) a novel data pipeline optimized for cloud-based parallelism and memory-efficient batch processing. LCSAI supports integration across transcriptomic, epigenomic, and spatial single-cell modalities, enabling scalable training on massive datasets while explicitly modeling domain shifts via adversarial domain discriminators.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the Human Cell Atlas (HCA) v1.0, Tabula Sapiens v2, and the ASAP spatial transcriptomics benchmark datasets; compare against baselines including scVI, Harmony, and scANVI. Metrics include adjusted Rand index (ARI) with >5% absolute improvement over baselines on cell type clustering accuracy, batch mixing entropy >0.1 increase indicating domain integration, and computational throughput measured by training time reduction >30% on equivalent cloud GPU nodes. Perform ablation studies on negative sampling strategies and domain adaptation modules across 5 random seeds.

NOVELTY: Unlike existing single-cell foundation models that scale poorly and inadequately correct batch effects across large, multi-institutional datasets, LCSAI uniquely integrates optimized cross-dataset negative sampling with domain-adaptive batch correction in a distributed cloud environment, enabling robust, scalable multi-modal atlas construction with preserved biological fidelity and significantly improved computational efficiency.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a hierarchical temporal graph neural network foundation model that explicitly models dynamic cellular interaction networks and evolving biological hierarchies from time-series single-cell multi-omics data, enabling interpretable, streaming analysis of cellular processes over time.

BACKGROUND: Current single-cell foundation models lack the capability to handle streaming time-series data while capturing temporal dependencies in cellular interaction networks, limiting our understanding of dynamic biological processes such as differentiation, signaling cascades, and disease progression. Furthermore, existing models do not explicitly integrate hierarchical biological relationships (e.g., cell types, lineages, tissues) in a temporally coherent manner, which is critical for interpretability and mechanistic insights.

APPROACH: We propose a hierarchical temporal graph neural network (HT-GNN) architecture that operates on time-resolved single-cell data, integrating multi-omic measurements (e.g., transcriptomics, epigenomics) as node features and modeling cell-cell interactions as evolving edges. The model employs a streaming graph update mechanism with temporal attention layers to capture dynamic changes, coupled with hierarchical pooling and embedding modules to explicitly represent biological hierarchies from single cells up to tissue-level structures. Training leverages self-supervised objectives including temporal contrastive learning and hierarchical consistency losses to ensure biologically meaningful latent representations.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate on benchmark time-series single-cell datasets including the Mouse Embryonic Development Atlas (scRNA-seq timepoints), the Human Immune Cell Dynamics dataset (10x Multiome ATAC + RNA), and the Tabula Muris Senis aging atlas with longitudinal samples. Baselines include scBERT, GraphSAGE, and ST-GCN. Metrics will focus on dynamic network reconstruction accuracy (AUPR and F1-score >5% improvement), temporal trajectory prediction error (mean squared error reduction by >10%), and hierarchical clustering adjusted Rand index (ARI improvement >7%). Experiments will run on Nvidia A100 GPUs with training budgets of ~100 GPU-hours per model, across 5 random seeds, with ablation studies on temporal attention and hierarchical pooling components.

NOVELTY: Unlike recent single-cell foundation models and graph neural networks—which do not jointly model streaming temporal graph dynamics and explicit biological hierarchies—our HT-GNN provides a unified, interpretable framework that captures evolving cellular networks at multiple biological scales, enabling new mechanistic insights into temporal cellular processes inaccessible to prior methods.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a temporal graph neural network (TGNN)-based foundation model that explicitly integrates irregular time-series constraints and developmental lineage priors to robustly infer and predict dynamic single-cell genomic trajectories across multiple developmental stages.

BACKGROUND: Current single-cell foundation models and graph neural networks lack robust mechanisms to handle the temporal continuity and irregular sampling intervals inherent in longitudinal single-cell genomics data, limiting their ability to accurately model cell state evolution and lineage differentiation over time. This open problem hinders precise reconstruction of developmental trajectories and dynamic gene regulation patterns from temporally sparse and noisy datasets.

APPROACH: We propose a novel Temporal Lineage-Aware Graph Neural Network (TLAGNN) framework that combines temporal point process modeling with graph attention layers to capture dynamic cellular interactions at irregular time points. TLAGNN incorporates prior knowledge of lineage hierarchies as graph constraints and integrates multi-omic genomic features in a multimodal embedding space, allowing end-to-end learning of temporal graph representations that reflect both genomic state transitions and lineage differentiation. The model will be pretrained on large-scale longitudinal single-cell atlases using masked temporal prediction and edge evolution tasks, then fine-tuned for trajectory inference and dynamic gene regulatory network reconstruction.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate TLAGNN on three benchmark longitudinal single-cell datasets with known developmental timepoints: (1) Mouse Gastrulation Atlas (Cao et al., 2019), (2) Human Hematopoiesis Time-series scRNA-seq (Laurenti & Göttgens, 2018), and (3) Zebrafish Embryogenesis scMultiome dataset. Baselines include CellRank, Palantir, and TrajectoryNet. Metrics will measure trajectory inference accuracy (Kendall’s Tau correlation >0.75 improvement over baselines), lineage assignment F1-score (>5% absolute gain), and dynamic gene regulatory network reconstruction AUPRC (>10% increase). Training will use 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs for 72 hours, with 5 random seeds per experiment and ablation studies on temporal regularization and lineage prior components.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior single-cell foundation models and trajectory inference methods that ignore temporal irregularity or rely on static graph embeddings, TLAGNN uniquely fuses temporal point process modeling with lineage-aware graph attention to explicitly model irregular sampling and lineage constraints, enabling precise dynamic trajectory learning and predictive power across diverse developmental systems.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a biologically informed, self-supervised genomic language model fine-tuned via meta-learning to reliably perform clinical diagnostics and prognostics on small, noisy single-cell RNA-seq datasets with minimal labeled data.

BACKGROUND: Clinical single-cell datasets are often limited in size and noisy, making it challenging for existing diagnostic models to generalize and maintain biological fidelity. Current foundation models pretrained on large-scale genomics data fail to adapt effectively to these low-data, high-stakes clinical settings, creating a critical gap between large-scale pretraining and domain-specific clinical applications. This bottleneck hinders the deployment of precision medicine tools that require robust, biologically interpretable predictions under data scarcity.

APPROACH: We propose a multi-stage framework combining (1) large-scale self-supervised pretraining of a transformer-based genomic language model on human and cross-species single-cell atlases (e.g., Tabula Sapiens, Human Cell Atlas) to capture biological priors, (2) meta-learning-based fine-tuning on limited labeled clinical datasets (e.g., COVID-19 PBMC single-cell RNA-seq, AML bone marrow scRNA-seq) to rapidly adapt to new diagnostic tasks, and (3) incorporation of biologically motivated regularization techniques such as pathway-aware attention masking and cell-type-informed contrastive loss to preserve interpretability and biological consistency.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the COVID-19 PBMC single-cell RNA-seq dataset, Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) bone marrow dataset, and the Human Tumor Atlas Network (HTAN) clinical single-cell datasets. Baselines include scBERT, scVI, and Nephrobase Cell+. Metrics: achieve >3% absolute improvement in F1 score on diagnostic classification, reduce false positive rate by >15%, and improve biological interpretability measured by pathway enrichment concordance (>10% increase). Conduct 5 independent runs with different random seeds, ablate meta-learning and biological regularization components, using GPU clusters with 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs and total training budget of ~1,000 GPU-hours.

NOVELTY: Unlike existing single-cell foundation models that rely solely on large-scale pretraining or task-specific fine-tuning, this method uniquely integrates meta-learning and biologically grounded regularizations to enable few-shot adaptation and maintain interpretability in noisy clinical single-cell contexts. Prior work such as Nephrobase Cell+ does not explicitly address data scarcity adaptation or incorporate meta-learning for rapid tuning to clinical diagnostic tasks.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a decentralized, noise-resilient emergent communication framework for swarm robotics that enables on-the-fly symbolic protocol evolution with built-in error correction and adaptive stability under intermittent connectivity in unstructured physical environments.

BACKGROUND:  
Swarm robotics operating in unstructured, noisy environments suffer from unreliable communication due to limited sensing, intermittent links, and dynamic topology changes. Existing emergent communication methods fail to maintain stable symbolic protocols or correct communication errors rapidly in fully decentralized settings, which critically degrades coordination and task success in real-world scenarios. The open problem is enabling robust, adaptive communication protocols that evolve and persist reliably without centralized control despite noisy, dynamic conditions.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-layer communication framework combining (1) a decentralized probabilistic symbol evolution mechanism that adapts symbol vocabularies based on local noise estimations, (2) integrated lightweight error correction codes inspired by fountain codes tailored for intermittent connectivity, and (3) continual self-stabilization via reinforcement learning with inductive biases for both positive signalling and positive listening. Agents will leverage local perception and interaction history to collaboratively refine and propagate robust symbolic protocols that tolerate noise and link failures without global synchronization.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the Swarm Robotics Unstructured Environment Benchmark (S-RUEB) dataset featuring real-world simulation logs, the OpenAI Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE) extended with physical noise models, and the RoboCup Rescue Simulation benchmark. Compare against baselines including the Biases for Emergent Communication (2019), Neuro-spike Communication Framework (2025), and Firefly-inspired Vocabulary Generator (2022). Metrics: achieve >3% absolute improvement in task success rate, reduce communication error rate by 40%, and maintain protocol stability >90% over 1000 communication episodes. Experiments run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 seeds per setting; planned ablations on error correction codes and inductive bias components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that either assumes stable connectivity or lacks error correction, our method explicitly integrates adaptive symbol evolution with noise-aware error correction in a fully decentralized manner, ensuring robust emergent communication protocols that maintain stability and cooperation effectiveness in dynamic, noisy swarm robotics environments where previous frameworks fail.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hierarchical neuro-symbolic emergent communication framework with multi-level adaptive message encoding that dynamically adjusts both compression granularity and symbolic abstraction to optimize coordination quality under intermittent and heterogeneous connectivity in multi-agent disaster response and autonomous navigation tasks.

BACKGROUND:  
Intermittent and heterogeneous network connectivity in multi-agent systems, especially in disaster response scenarios, causes frequent communication failures, excessive latency, and loss of interpretability in emergent protocols, undermining coordination reliability. Existing neuro-symbolic adaptive encoding approaches optimize message compression based on local connectivity quality but lack hierarchical abstraction across multiple semantic levels and do not accommodate heterogeneous agent capabilities or network modalities. This gap prevents robust, scalable emergent communication that simultaneously maximizes symbolic fidelity, resilience, and efficiency under realistic network conditions.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical neuro-symbolic communication protocol combining three key components: (1) multi-level adaptive encoding that modulates message granularity from raw neural embeddings to high-level symbolic tokens based on real-time connectivity metrics, (2) heterogeneous agent-aware message adaptation that accounts for agent-specific decoding and computational constraints via personalized encoding modules, and (3) cross-modal redundancy injection using lightweight neuro-spike inspired signaling to maintain synchronization and reduce error in highly intermittent links. The system dynamically balances symbolic interpretability with neural adaptability through reinforcement learning optimizing coordination reward under varying network outages.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on three benchmark datasets: RoboRescue (disaster response coordination with intermittent connectivity), Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MAPE) navigation tasks with communication constraints, and the Google Research Football dataset (multi-agent teamwork with dynamic communication topology). Baselines include: Neuro-Symbolic Protocols with Adaptive Message Encoding (tournament winner), Biases for Emergent Communication (2019), and Neuro-spike Communication Framework (2025). Metrics measured are task completion time (aiming for >15% reduction vs baselines), message delivery rate (>10% absolute improvement), and symbolic fidelity quantified by token reconstruction accuracy (>5% absolute improvement). Experimental protocol involves training on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of ~10,000 GPU hours, conducting experiments over 5 random seeds, and performing ablation studies on encoding hierarchy depth, agent heterogeneity settings, and redundancy injection mechanisms.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, this approach introduces a hierarchical multi-level encoding strategy that concurrently optimizes symbolic abstraction and neural compression tailored to heterogeneous agents and network conditions, and uniquely integrates neuro-spike inspired redundancy signaling for synchronization under severe intermittent connectivity. This enables robust, interpretable emergent communication protocols that adapt dynamically to both agent and network heterogeneity, beyond single-level adaptive encoding or fixed-format neuro-spike communication.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a robust emergent communication framework for multi-agent edge computing that explicitly handles intermittent connectivity, asynchronous messaging, and dynamic network topologies by integrating a neural communication protocol with adaptive error-correction, temporal message encoding, and topology-aware routing.

BACKGROUND:  
Current emergent communication protocols for multi-agent systems assume stable network conditions and synchronous message exchange, which leads to brittle coordination and failures in real-world edge computing environments characterized by frequent disconnections, packet loss, and constantly changing network topologies. There is an open problem in designing communication methods that maintain reliability and efficiency despite such intermittent and dynamic connectivity, which existing approaches fail to address jointly.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-component neural communication channel that incorporates (1) adaptive forward error correction codes learned end-to-end to mitigate packet loss, (2) temporal encoding schemes that allow asynchronous message alignment and aggregation without strict synchronization, and (3) a topology-aware communication policy module that dynamically adjusts message routing and agent communication partners based on real-time connectivity graphs. These components are trained jointly via multi-agent reinforcement learning in simulation environments modeling realistic edge network conditions with stochastic disconnections and variable wireless noise.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will benchmark on the following datasets/environments: the Wireless Network Simulator (WiNeS) dataset for intermittent connectivity traces, the EdgeDroid benchmark for dynamic mobile edge computing scenarios, and the Multi-agent Particle Environment (MPE) adapted with noisy and intermittent channels. Baselines include DIAL (Differentiable Inter-Agent Learning), CommNet, and Neuro-spike communication frameworks. Evaluation metrics will be task success rate (target >10% absolute improvement over baselines), communication overhead (target reduction of ≥25% in bits transmitted), and latency (reduce message delivery time by ≥20% under packet loss conditions). Experiments will run on GPU clusters with 3 seeds per setup, include ablations on error correction, synchronization, and topology modules, and training budgets capped at 48 hours per model.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior emergent communication work that assumes stable, synchronous networks or focuses on either low-bandwidth communication or fixed topologies, this method explicitly co-designs robust error correction, asynchronous temporal encoding, and dynamic topology adaptation within a single end-to-end learned communication framework, enabling reliable multi-agent coordination under real edge computing conditions of intermittent connectivity and network variability.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a latency-constrained, concurrent neuro-symbolic communication framework with adaptive compression for real-time emergent coordination in drone swarms, explicitly optimizing message encoding and symbolic reasoning pipelines to achieve sub-50ms communication delays while preserving interpretability and task success.

BACKGROUND:  
Current neuro-symbolic communication protocols in drone swarms incur latency primarily due to sequential symbolic reasoning layers, which critically hinder responsiveness during fast, safety-critical maneuvers. Existing methods either sacrifice interpretability for speed or fail to scale to real-time constraints, leaving an open problem in designing communication systems that are simultaneously interpretable, low-latency, and robust for emergent multi-agent coordination.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid communication protocol integrating neural message encoding and symbolic reasoning in a parallelized pipeline, combined with an adaptive message compression module that dynamically reduces communication payload based on contextual urgency. Symbolic reasoning operates concurrently with neural encoding, enabling immediate partial interpretability without introducing blocking delays. The system employs reinforcement learning for emergent protocol optimization under strict latency constraints and utilizes a neuro-symbolic fusion architecture tailored for drone swarm control commands and state sharing.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the AirSim drone swarm dataset, the Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE), and the OpenAI RoboSumo environment to cover real-world and simulated multi-agent coordination tasks. Metrics include average communication delay (target <50ms), coordination success rate (>90%), and interpretability measured via symbolic message accuracy (>85%). Baselines include Neuro-Symbolic Communication (NSC) by Chen et al. (2023), Pure Neural Emergent Communication (PN-EC) from Foerster et al. (2016), and Latency-Optimized Symbolic Messaging (LOS-M) by Singh et al. (2024). Experiments run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds per setting and ablation studies on compression and concurrency modules.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works that treat symbolic and neural components sequentially or use fixed compression schemes, our approach achieves concurrent neuro-symbolic processing with adaptive compression to guarantee ultra-low latency emergent communication while maintaining interpretability and high coordination success, a capability not demonstrated by existing methods such as NSC or PN-EC.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:
