Develop an evolutionary framework for robust emergent communication protocols in swarm robotics that explicitly optimize for intermittent connectivity and ultra-low bandwidth constraints in disaster response scenarios, enabling resilient coordinated behaviors under severe real-world communication limitations.

BACKGROUND:  
Current emergent communication methods for multi-agent systems typically assume stable connectivity and moderate bandwidth, conditions rarely met in disaster response environments characterized by intermittent, unreliable communication channels and strict energy constraints. This leads to fragile coordination and degraded mission outcomes, such as incomplete search and rescue coverage. The open problem is to create communication protocols that evolve to be inherently robust against severe connectivity disruptions and bandwidth scarcity, ensuring reliable collective behavior in real-world disaster scenarios.

APPROACH:  
We propose an evolutionary language emergence method that integrates a multi-objective optimization framework balancing communication robustness, bandwidth efficiency, and behavioral coordination effectiveness. Agents co-evolve discrete, minimalistic communication vocabularies and adaptive retransmission strategies sensitive to packet loss and delays, guided by reward signals tied to mission success metrics in simulated disaster environments. We leverage neuro-spike inspired signaling to minimize payload size while evolving error-tolerant coding schemes that enable message reconstruction under intermittent links. The evolutionary process operates on a population of agent policies and communication protocols, jointly optimizing for resilience to connectivity dropouts and strict bandwidth caps.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on the DARPA Subterranean Challenge Dataset for search and rescue simulation scenarios, the RoboCup Rescue Simulation platform, and the Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE) adapted with stochastic communication dropouts. Baselines include the Swarm Communication Protocol (SCP) from [Firefly-inspired vocabulary generator, 2022], the neuro-spike communication framework [2025], and the inductive bias approach from [Biases for Emergent Communication, 2019]. Metrics include >10% absolute improvement in mission completion rate under 50% packet loss, reduction of bandwidth usage by at least 40% versus SCP, and coordination latency decreased by 20%. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds and include ablations on communication vocabulary size, retransmission strategy, and evolutionary population diversity.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that either focuses on inductive biases for idealized environments or neuro-spike efficiency without explicit resilience mechanisms, our method is the first to jointly evolve communication protocols and agent policies specifically to withstand unpredictable connectivity outages and extreme bandwidth constraints in disaster response. This direct optimization for robustness under real-world constraints enables practical deployment in scenarios where prior emergent communication systems fail.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a lightweight, on-device mechanistic interpretability framework for transformer models that performs efficient causal mediation analysis under strict latency and compute constraints of real-time edge devices using compressed activation interventions and sparse causal attribution.

BACKGROUND:  
Current mechanistic interpretability methods rely on full or near-complete activation data and intensive computation, making them infeasible for real-time deployment on edge devices with limited resources and noisy partial observability. This gap prevents trustworthy, causally-grounded explanations from being generated on-device in latency-sensitive applications such as mobile NLP or IoT sensor data processing. The open problem is how to achieve accurate causal attributions and mechanistic insights without offloading computation to the cloud or sacrificing explanation fidelity under severe resource constraints.

APPROACH:  
We propose an efficient causal mediation analysis framework tailored for transformer models on edge hardware that (1) compresses activation data via learned sparse probes that identify minimal intervention subsets per layer and head, (2) employs lightweight causal effect estimators optimized for noisy, partial activation observations, and (3) integrates a binarized intervention policy to reduce overhead during attribution computation. This framework runs entirely on-device using low-bit quantized transformer checkpoints and exploits model structural priors to prune candidate causal paths dynamically, significantly lowering runtime and memory usage.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on GLUE (MNLI, SST-2), Penn Treebank (for language modeling), and Speech Commands v2 (time-series transformer) datasets with BERT-base, DistilBERT, and Transformer-XL baselines. Measure causal attribution quality (Average Treatment Effect accuracy) with a target >10% improvement over baselines that use partial activations, reduce explanation latency by >30%, and maintain memory use under 100MB. Perform ablations on intervention sparsity levels and quantization schemes, with 5 random seeds and edge compute simulated via Raspberry Pi 4 hardware.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work which assumes full activation access and cloud-level compute (e.g., singular vector decomposition or residual stream analyses), this method enables principled mechanistic causal interpretability directly on edge devices with partial/noisy data and strict latency, a setting no current method adequately addresses. It advances interpretability from theoretical insights toward deployment in real-time, resource-constrained environments.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a formally verifiable symbolic circuit extraction framework that produces certified, minimal-complexity symbolic programs for transformer models to enable legally and ethically mandated interpretability in safety-critical systems.

BACKGROUND:  
Current symbolic circuit extraction techniques for mechanistic interpretability of transformers lack formal correctness guarantees and often generate overly complex, uninterpretable representations that impede certification and deployment in safety-critical domains such as autonomous driving and medical diagnosis. This prevents leveraging mechanistic interpretability to meet strict legal and ethical requirements for explainability and formal reliability in these high-stakes settings.

APPROACH:  
We propose a formal symbolic circuit extraction pipeline that integrates symbolic simplification with automated theorem proving to produce minimal, formally verifiable symbolic programs representing transformer model internals. The method incorporates complexity-regularized symbolic program synthesis constrained by formal correctness properties derived from model specifications and safety requirements. Verification conditions are automatically generated and checked using SMT solvers to guarantee that the extracted symbolic program faithfully represents the model’s computation with provable bounds on approximation error. This balance between symbolic complexity and formal verification enables certified mechanistic interpretability tailored for transformer-based safety-critical systems.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the Safety-Critical Autonomous Driving dataset (Argoverse 2), the MIMIC-IV clinical dataset for medical diagnosis, and the GLUE benchmark for NLP safety tasks. Baselines include BERT-base, GPT-3, and the existing Symbolic Circuit Extraction method from prior MI literature. Metrics are (1) formal verification success rate (target >95%), (2) symbolic program size reduction (target ≥50% reduction vs baseline symbolic representations), and (3) fidelity measured by functional equivalence or bounded error with original transformer (target ≤2% accuracy drop). Experiments run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs over 5 seeds, including ablations on complexity regularization and verification strictness.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works that focus on interpretability without formal correctness guarantees or produce complex symbolic programs unsuitable for certification, our approach is the first to simultaneously optimize symbolic circuit simplicity and provide formal, machine-checked verification guarantees, enabling certified mechanistic interpretability for safety-critical transformer applications.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Integrate formal verification and symbolic program extraction with causal mechanistic interpretability to produce provably correct, auditable symbolic programs that faithfully represent transformer decision processes in safety-critical healthcare applications.

BACKGROUND:  
Current mechanistic interpretability techniques for transformer models lack formal correctness guarantees and are insufficiently rigorous for deployment in regulated domains such as healthcare, where certification demands provable model safety and transparency. Existing symbolic extraction methods generate interpretable programs but without formal verification, limiting trustworthiness. There is an urgent need to produce mechanistic explanations that are both interpretable and formally verified against domain-specific safety specifications.

APPROACH:  
We propose a pipeline that first applies causal mechanistic interpretability methods to identify critical model components and causal dependencies within healthcare-oriented transformer models (e.g., clinical note classifiers). Next, we extract symbolic program representations of these components and their interactions using enhanced symbolic extraction algorithms. Finally, we integrate automated theorem proving and model checking frameworks to formally verify that the extracted symbolic programs satisfy predefined safety-critical properties (e.g., fairness, error bounds, medically relevant invariants). The approach also includes an interpretability audit module that cross-validates the symbolic programs’ alignment with model behavior on held-out clinical datasets.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate the approach on transformer models fine-tuned on MIMIC-III, ClinicalBERT, and eICU benchmark datasets. Baselines include ClinicalBERT, BioBERT, and RoBERTa-base fine-tuned on the same tasks. Metrics include (1) formal verification success rate (percentage of safety properties proven correct), (2) fidelity of symbolic programs to model outputs (target >95% alignment), and (3) interpretability audit scores (human expert rating on a 5-point scale, aiming for >4). Ablation studies will isolate the effect of formal verification integration and causal component selection. Experiments will use 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds per configuration to ensure statistical significance.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work which either focuses solely on symbolic extraction without formal guarantees or mechanistic interpretability without provable correctness, this method uniquely combines causal mechanistic analysis with formal verification techniques to generate symbolic programs that are both interpretable and certifiably safe for healthcare model deployment, a capability absent in existing transformer interpretability research.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop Dynamic Sparse Manifold Tracking (DSMT), a method that leverages temporal graph neural networks to continuously identify and track evolving geometric manifolds within sparse transformer activations across layers and timesteps, thereby enabling precise mechanistic interpretability of sparse transformer dynamics beyond static clustering approaches.

BACKGROUND:  
Current geometric decomposition and clustering techniques fail to robustly identify meaningful activation manifolds in sparse transformers due to irregular and temporally varying activation and weight sparsity. Existing methods, including Sparse Activation Manifold Identification via Adaptive Clustering with Dynamic Connectivity, rely on static or piecewise clustering that cannot capture the continuous evolution and temporal dependencies of sparse activation patterns across layers and inputs. This limits mechanistic interpretability and understanding of how sparse transformers encode and process information dynamically.

APPROACH:  
DSMT models sparse transformer activations as dynamic graphs where nodes represent activation units and edges represent evolving connectivity patterns inferred from attention weights and co-activation statistics. A temporal graph neural network (TGNN) is trained to adaptively cluster these nodes into manifolds that evolve smoothly over layers and timesteps, explicitly modeling temporal continuity and sparse connectivity changes. This approach incorporates attention sparsity masks and activation magnitudes as node and edge features, enabling robust manifold tracking despite irregular sparsity. Additionally, DSMT includes a disentanglement regularizer that encourages orthogonality and semantic coherence of discovered manifolds for interpretability.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate DSMT on transformer models trained on three established benchmarks: LongRangeArena (LRA), GLUE/SuperGLUE for language understanding, and ESC-50 for time-series and audio classification with sparse transformer variants. Baselines include Sparse Activation Manifold Identification via Adaptive Clustering with Dynamic Connectivity, standard spectral clustering, and Singular Vector-Based Interpretability of Transformer Circuits. Metrics include manifold purity (>85% target), temporal clustering stability (adjusted Rand index >0.8 across layers), interpretability scores based on human-annotated feature alignment, and downstream task accuracy impact (<1% degradation). Experiments will run on A100 GPUs with a compute budget of 5k GPU hours, using 5 random seeds per setup with ablations on graph feature sets and regularizer strength.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike previous static or piecewise clustering methods, DSMT explicitly models the continuous temporal evolution of sparse activation manifolds using TGNNs, capturing dynamic connectivity patterns that prior work cannot. This enables discovery of temporally coherent, semantically meaningful manifold trajectories across transformer layers and timesteps, providing a finer-grained and temporally aware mechanistic interpretability of sparse transformer internals.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an Incremental Sparse Causal Mediation framework with hierarchical intervention prioritization for real-time mechanistic interpretability of transformer models on edge devices under strict latency and compute constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing causal mediation methods for transformer interpretability require full-batch processing and dense interventions, making them infeasible for streaming or edge-device applications where latency budgets are under 50ms and compute resources are limited. Current approaches lack mechanisms to dynamically prioritize sparse, high-impact interventions incrementally, preventing real-time generation of faithful causal effect maps necessary for actionable transparency in deployed transformer systems.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel Incremental Sparse Causal Estimation (ISCE) method that incrementally updates sparse causal mediation estimates by exploiting hierarchical intervention prioritization—first identifying coarse-grained causal bottlenecks and then refining with fine-grained interventions only where causal uncertainty remains high. This method leverages lightweight approximations of mediator variables tailored for transformer edge architectures and streaming input scenarios. It integrates dynamically adaptive thresholds to enforce intervention sparsity and uses incremental updates to maintain interpretability fidelity without batch recomputation.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on GLUE and SuperGLUE benchmarks adapted to streaming on-device settings, and additionally on Penn Treebank for language modeling interpretability. Baselines include BERT-base with batch causal mediation, LoRA for parameter efficiency, and AdamW optimizer with dense intervention protocols. Metrics are: (1) latency per input under 50ms on ARM-based edge hardware; (2) intervention sparsity ratio (targeting >70% reduction in interventions vs dense methods); (3) causal effect estimation fidelity with an expected <5% deviation from offline ground-truth causal maps; and (4) downstream task accuracy within 1% of baseline. Experimental protocol involves 3 seeds per setup, with ablations on hierarchical prioritization and thresholding strategies, using a maximum compute budget equivalent to 1 TPUv3 core for 48 hours.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior mechanistic interpretability and causal mediation approaches that only support dense, batch offline analyses (e.g., “Incremental Sparse Causal Estimation for Edge Transformers” baseline or recent sparse autoencoder methods), this framework uniquely combines incremental update mechanics with hierarchical, adaptive sparse interventions, enabling faithful, real-time causal interpretability on edge devices—a capability absent from all referenced SOTA works including streaming adaptations or dynamical systems perspectives.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA: Develop a resource-efficient, incremental algorithm for real-time persistent homology embedding updates on streaming sensor data, enabling low-latency topological analysis for anomaly detection in sensor networks.

BACKGROUND: Current topological data analysis methods for high-dimensional data, such as Mapper and batch persistent homology computations, lack the ability to incrementally and efficiently update persistence diagrams under strict latency and memory constraints. This creates bottlenecks in real-time sensor network applications, where continuous, low-latency anomaly detection and pattern recognition are critical but computational resources are limited. The open problem is designing incremental persistence computations that balance accuracy, computational overhead, and memory use in streaming, high-dimensional data environments.

APPROACH: We propose an incremental persistent homology embedding framework that updates persistence diagrams and topological summaries on streaming sensor inputs without recomputing from scratch. Key technical components include: (1) an edge-centric update strategy integrated with dynamic simplicial complex maintenance, (2) a compressed representation of persistence pairs to reduce memory footprint, and (3) a latency-aware scheduling algorithm that adapts update granularity based on available computational resources and real-time constraints. This approach supports high-dimensional time-series sensor data by leveraging dimension reduction techniques aligned with topological features, ensuring fidelity while optimizing compute and memory usage.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluation will be conducted on the Intel Lab Data (real-world sensor network for environmental monitoring), the NOAA Climate Sensor Dataset, and the UCI Gas Sensor Array Drift Dataset. Baselines include Batch Persistent Homology (Ripser), Incremental Persistent Homology (Gudhi’s dynamic updates), and Mapper-based streaming adaptations. Metrics will include latency reduction (>30% improvement), memory usage reduction (>25%), and topological accuracy measured by bottleneck distance (<0.05 absolute difference from batch baseline). Experiments will run on embedded-class hardware (ARM Cortex-A processors), with 5 repeated trials and ablation studies on update frequency and compression thresholds.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work such as Mapper or static persistence computations, this method enables true incremental, latency-sensitive updates of persistence embeddings suitable for resource-constrained streaming sensor data. It fills a critical gap by explicitly balancing computational efficiency and topological fidelity in real-time, which no existing topological method currently supports for high-dimensional streaming sensor networks.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA: Develop an adaptive, streaming-capable filtration framework for topological data analysis that enables real-time, incremental updating of topological summaries on resource-constrained edge devices for high-dimensional IoT sensor data.

BACKGROUND: Current topological data analysis (TDA) methods for high-dimensional data typically rely on batch processing and require substantial computational resources, making them unsuitable for real-time deployment on edge devices with strict latency and memory limitations. Existing filtration learning techniques cannot incrementally update persistent homology or Mapper-like structures efficiently under streaming constraints, blocking timely anomaly detection and decision-making in IoT sensor networks.

APPROACH: We propose a lightweight, incremental filtration learning algorithm that dynamically updates simplicial complexes or cover constructions on streaming high-dimensional data with bounded memory and computation. Key components include (1) a memory-efficient sparse representation of filtrations using adaptive landmark-based covers, (2) an incremental update scheme for persistence diagrams via localized simplex additions/removals, and (3) an edge-computing optimized heuristic to balance topological fidelity and resource constraints by adjusting filtration resolution on-the-fly. This framework will be implemented using low-overhead data structures suitable for microcontroller-class hardware.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on real-world IoT sensor datasets: (1) Intel Lab Data (sensor network environmental measurements), (2) UCI Gas Sensor Array Drift Dataset, and (3) NASA Turbofan Engine Degradation Simulation Dataset. Baselines include Mapper (Singh et al., 2007), Dynamic Persistent Homology methods such as DIONYSUS incremental updates, and the Real-time Edge Computing TDA filtration model from the winning proposal. Metrics will be: (a) latency per update (<50 ms target), (b) memory footprint (<50 MB RAM), (c) topological accuracy measured by bottleneck distance to offline batch persistent homology (<0.1 threshold), and (d) anomaly detection F1 score improvement (aiming >5% absolute gain over baselines). Experiments will run on ARM Cortex-M7 class hardware with 10 repeated seeds for statistical significance and include ablations on filtration resolution and update frequency.

NOVELTY: Unlike Mapper and existing batch or offline nonlinear algebraic topology methods, our approach supports continuous, incremental filtration learning with provable topological fidelity guarantees on streaming high-dimensional data under strict edge device constraints. Prior real-time edge TDA work lacks formal incremental topological update mechanisms and does not adaptively balance resource constraints with topological accuracy, which our method directly addresses.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable, distributed adaptive multi-parameter filtration framework integrating robust noise modeling and biologically informed priors to enable interpretable topological summaries of extremely high-dimensional, noisy genomic datasets at population scale.

BACKGROUND:  
Current topological data analysis (TDA) methods for genomic data, including Mapper and Ball Mapper, suffer from limited scalability and robustness when applied to population-scale datasets with millions of samples and thousands of features, compounded by missing values and biological noise. Existing approaches lack principled adaptive filtration schemes that simultaneously scale across compute resources and incorporate biological constraints, thereby failing to capture subtle topological features critical for understanding complex genetic variation and functional genomics.

APPROACH:  
We propose a distributed computation framework leveraging Apache Spark and GPU acceleration for scalable construction of multi-parameter persistent homology filtrations, coupled with an adaptive filtration parameter optimization module that incorporates robust noise models and integrates biologically informed priors (e.g., gene regulatory networks, known pathways) to guide filtration parameter selection. The method employs a novel robust distance metric combining imputation uncertainty and biological similarity, enabling filtration adaptation that preserves meaningful topological signals despite missing or noisy data. The framework outputs interpretable topological summaries (e.g., persistence landscapes, multiparameter Betti curves) linked to biological annotations for downstream analysis.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three real large-scale genomic datasets: 1) The 1000 Genomes Project phase 3 variant dataset (∼2,500 individuals, ∼80 million variants), 2) GTEx v8 RNA-seq expression profiles across multiple tissues (∼17,000 samples, ∼56,000 genes), and 3) UK Biobank WGS data subset (∼50,000 individuals, ∼10 million variants). Baselines include Mapper, Ball Mapper, and the Scalable Adaptive Filtration method from the tournament winner paper. Quantitatively assess topological feature stability (bottleneck distance variance <0.05), biological relevance (enrichment of detected features in known gene sets, >10% improvement in enrichment p-values vs baselines), and computational scalability (reduce runtime by ≥30% vs tournament winner method) on identical hardware (multi-node cluster with ≥8 GPUs). Perform ablation studies on noise model components and priors, running each experiment with 5 random seeds to assess robustness.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods that either lack scalability or robustness to noise and missing data, our approach uniquely combines distributed multi-parameter filtration with adaptive noise-aware parameter tuning informed by biological priors, enabling discovery of subtle and biologically interpretable topological structures at unprecedented genomic data scales—something no existing TDA method currently achieves.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable, parallelized multi-scale persistent homology framework integrating adaptive noise filtering with topology-aware deep feature extraction to robustly characterize complex biological heterogeneity in ultra-high-dimensional single-cell and spatial omics datasets.

BACKGROUND:  
Current topological data analysis (TDA) methods like parallelized persistent homology achieve scalability and noise robustness on large genomic cohorts (e.g., TCGA), but they fail to integrate rich feature representations from high-dimensional single-cell and spatial omics data, which exhibit complex cell-type heterogeneity and spatial dependencies. Moreover, prior approaches lack multi-scale adaptive noise filtering that dynamically adjusts to local data density and biological variance, limiting the biological relevance of extracted topological features in spatially-resolved single-cell contexts.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel pipeline combining (1) parallelized multi-parameter persistent homology computation optimized for HPC clusters, (2) adaptive, topology-driven noise filtering that dynamically modulates filtration scales based on local data topology and density, and (3) integration of topology-aware deep feature embeddings generated by graph neural networks trained on spatial and single-cell RNA-seq data modalities. This approach jointly optimizes noise robustness and topological feature expressiveness while scaling to millions of cells and spatial spots. The method incorporates a multi-scale filtration that automatically adapts to intrinsic biological heterogeneity and spatial autocorrelation, producing biologically meaningful persistent homology summaries amenable to downstream subtype classification and spatial pattern identification.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark datasets: (1) 10x Genomics single-cell RNA-seq (PBMC 68k), (2) Human Cell Atlas spatial transcriptomics datasets (e.g., Slide-seq), and (3) TCGA Pan-Cancer Atlas bulk and spatial genomics data. Baselines include Parallelized Persistent Homology with Robust Noise Filtering (the winning method), Mapper, and Ball Mapper. Quantitatively measure: (a) runtime speedup on HPC clusters aiming for >3x improvement over prior parallel methods, (b) biological relevance via adjusted Rand index (ARI) and silhouette scores for subtype clustering with expected >5% absolute improvement, and (c) spatial autocorrelation preservation via Moran’s I metric showing >10% gain over baselines. Conduct ablations on noise filtering strategies and embedding models with at least 5 random seeds for statistical robustness.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work—which addresses either scalability/noise robustness (Parallelized Persistent Homology) or topological summaries of bulk/low-dimensional data (Mapper, Ball Mapper)—this method uniquely integrates topology-aware deep embeddings with adaptive multi-parameter filtrations that respond dynamically to local biological complexity and spatial structure, enabling unprecedented robust topological analysis at single-cell and spatial omics scale.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA: Develop a Latency-Optimized Incremental Persistent Homology (LOIPH) framework that delivers real-time, sub-50ms streaming topological feature updates on resource-constrained edge devices for high-dimensional data analysis.

BACKGROUND: Current persistent homology algorithms either operate in batch mode or use incremental methods that suffer from high latency or excessive computational resource demands, making them unsuitable for real-time analysis on low-power edge hardware. This bottleneck limits applications in domains requiring immediate topological feature extraction from streaming high-dimensional sensor data, such as environmental monitoring or biomedical signals.

APPROACH: We propose a streaming persistent homology algorithm that incrementally updates Vietoris-Rips complexes via a latency-bounded edge collapse strategy combined with dynamic memory management to maintain homology stability under fixed CPU and memory budgets. The framework integrates an adaptive filtration selector to prioritize topological features most relevant to current data distribution, ensuring sub-50ms update latency on low-power ARM Cortex-A53-class processors. The method supports high-dimensional input by leveraging dimension reduction with topological guarantees before incremental homology computation.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate LOIPH on three real benchmark datasets: Intel Lab Environmental Sensor Data, UCI Gas Sensor Array Drift Dataset, and NOAA Climate Data Record. Baselines include Ripser, Dionysus, and the Streaming Persistent Homology method by Chen et al. (2023). Metrics will measure update latency (target <50ms), homology accuracy (persistent Betti number difference <5% vs batch), and resource consumption (CPU under 30% load, memory <200MB). Experiments will run on Raspberry Pi 4B and NVIDIA Jetson Nano with 5 independent runs and ablation studies on edge collapse thresholds and filtration schemes.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior batch or incremental homology algorithms (Ripser, Dionysus, Chen et al.), LOIPH guarantees strict upper bounds on update latency under fixed resource constraints while maintaining stable homological accuracy on streaming high-dimensional data directly on edge devices, enabling new real-time topological analytics applications impossible with previous methods.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a contrastive disentanglement framework that explicitly separates weather-invariant driving-relevant features from weather-variant nuisances in multi-modal autonomous driving data, enabling zero-shot generalization to unseen weather conditions in reinforcement learning policies for safe decision-making.

BACKGROUND:  
Current autonomous driving systems trained on data from specific weather conditions fail to generalize zero-shot to novel, unseen weather scenarios, causing perception and control failures that endanger safety. Existing representation learning methods conflate weather-variant visual and sensor noise with task-relevant features, degrading policy robustness under distribution shifts. The open problem is how to learn representations that disentangle and isolate environment-invariant driving features to enable zero-shot generalization to diverse, unseen weather conditions.

APPROACH:  
We propose Weather-Invariant Contrastive Disentanglement (WICD), a reinforcement learning framework that jointly trains a multi-modal encoder with a novel contrastive loss that pulls together embeddings of the same driving scenario under different weather conditions while pushing apart embeddings of different driving contexts. The model incorporates modality-specific encoders (camera, LiDAR, radar) and a disentanglement module that explicitly separates weather-invariant latent factors from weather-dependent nuisances, supervised by a weather classification adversary to enforce invariance. The RL policy is then trained exclusively on the disentangled invariant features, ensuring robust decision-making.  

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the CARLA Autonomous Driving Benchmark under varying weather conditions (Clear, Rain, Fog, Snow) plus the Oxford RobotCar Dataset and nuScenes Dataset for multi-modal sensor data diversity. Baselines include Contrastive Predictive Coding (CPC), MoCo, and Domain-Adversarial Neural Networks (DANN) for invariant feature learning, plus state-of-the-art RL policies trained without weather disentanglement. Metrics: Improvement of >5% absolute in zero-shot success rate on unseen weather conditions, reduction by 20% in collision rate, and >10% improvement in average episode return versus baselines. Training is performed on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs over 500k steps with 5 random seeds; ablations include removal of the adversarial weather classifier and modality-specific encoder fusion.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that integrates contextual clues or pessimistic policy evaluation, WICD explicitly disentangles weather-invariant driving features across multiple sensor modalities via contrastive learning and adversarial invariance, enabling zero-shot RL generalization to unseen weather conditions without any fine-tuning. This disentanglement approach is novel in combining multi-modal contrastive learning with policy training for autonomous driving zero-shot generalization, a gap not addressed by existing SOTA methods.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hierarchical multi-modal semantic task embedding framework that explicitly disentangles universal urban driving concepts from city-specific contextual factors using contrastive and causal representation learning, enabling robust zero-shot generalization of autonomous driving policies across highly heterogeneous urban environments.

BACKGROUND:  
Current zero-shot autonomous driving approaches fail to fully separate transferable semantic knowledge from city-specific contextual nuances, which limits their ability to generalize to unseen urban layouts with drastically different road structures, traffic rules, and sensor distributions. Existing transformer-based multi-modal embeddings capture rich joint features but lack explicit mechanisms to isolate and adapt to new environmental contexts without fine-tuning. This impedes safe deployment of autonomous driving policies in novel cities with unseen semantic and contextual variations.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical embedding model that first learns a universal semantic task embedding from multi-modal sensor data (LiDAR, cameras, HD maps) representing common urban driving concepts (e.g., intersections, traffic signals, pedestrian crossings) using contrastive learning across multiple cities. Concurrently, a city-specific context encoder models local variations via causal disentanglement techniques that identify invariant semantic features versus mutable contextual factors (e.g., traffic flow patterns, right vs left driving). An attention-based fusion module then dynamically conditions the policy on both disentangled embeddings, enabling zero-shot adaptation to unseen urban environments by recombining universal semantic knowledge with new city contexts without additional training. The policy is trained end-to-end with hierarchical reinforcement learning to optimize both universal task understanding and context adaptation components.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will leverage large-scale autonomous driving datasets with diverse urban settings: nuScenes, Argoverse, and Lyft Level 5. Baselines include the original Multi-Modal Semantic Task Embedding transformer, Inferring Behavior-Specific Context (2024), and Provable Zero-Shot Generalization methods (2025). Metrics will measure zero-shot driving policy success rate (>10% absolute improvement expected), embedding transferability via cross-city retrieval accuracy (>15% improvement), and safety-critical event reduction (>20% fewer infractions) in zero-shot scenarios. Experiments will be run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 seeds per setting and include ablations isolating hierarchical embedding components and disentanglement modules.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that jointly models multi-modal semantic features without explicit factorization, our method uniquely disentangles transferable semantic knowledge from mutable city contexts via causal representation learning and hierarchical embeddings, enabling dynamic zero-shot policy adaptation to drastically different urban environments without retraining or fine-tuning. This explicit factorization and fusion approach addresses the core limitation in current zero-shot urban driving frameworks, which do not handle contextual heterogeneity at this granularity or adapt dynamically at inference time.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA: Integrate noise-adaptive causal representation learning with context-aware latent dynamics modeling to enable stable zero-shot generalization of control policies under unpredictable sensorimotor noise and environment variation.

BACKGROUND: Current zero-shot generalization (ZSG) methods in reinforcement learning fail to robustly disentangle causal factors from noisy, variable sensorimotor streams in real-time robotic control, leading to brittle policies that degrade under hardware noise and dynamic disturbances. Existing approaches like noise-adaptive filtering or context inference either treat noise and causality separately or lack integration with latent dynamics, limiting their ability to generalize without fine-tuning. The open problem is how to jointly and efficiently infer stable causal latent factors and adapt control policies in real time to achieve robust zero-shot generalization under both environment shifts and sensor noise.

APPROACH: We propose Noise-Adaptive Contextual Causal Dynamics (NACCD), a unified framework that combines adaptive Kalman filtering integrated into a causal representation learner with a context-aware latent dynamics model conditioned on inferred environment and noise context variables. NACCD uses an online noise-adaptive filtering module that updates causal latent variables with latency under 50ms, while simultaneously encoding environment context to modulate the latent dynamics predictive model. This joint inference enables stable disentanglement of physical causality from noise-adaptive embeddings, which are then fed into a policy network trained via offline reinforcement learning with pessimistic risk minimization to ensure robust zero-shot generalization. The entire system is end-to-end differentiable and optimized for real-time control.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate NACCD on 1) Meta-World benchmark for real robot manipulation tasks, 2) OpenAI Gym MuJoCo continuous control tasks with injected sensor noise, and 3) the Real-World Robot Arm Dataset (e.g., from the Robot Learning Lab at UC Berkeley). Compare against baselines Real-time Causal Inference with Noise-Adaptive Filtering (the tournament winner), PERM and PPPO from Provable Zero-Shot Generalization in Offline RL, and Inferring Behavior-Specific Context methods. Metrics include: causal inference accuracy (>10% absolute improvement in disentanglement score), control policy zero-shot success rate (>15% absolute improvement on unseen tasks), computational latency (<50ms control loop overhead), and robustness to noise (performance degradation <5% under 30% injected sensor noise). Use 5 random seeds, ablation studies on noise adaptation and context conditioning, on hardware with GPUs and real-time robotic control setups under 100ms latency constraints.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior methods that either separately address noise-adaptive filtering or context-aware generalization, NACCD tightly fuses noise-adaptive filtering with causal latent dynamics conditioned on environment context, enabling stable, low-latency causal inference combined with robust zero-shot policy generalization in noisy real-world robotics. Previous approaches do not jointly optimize causal disentanglement, noise robustness, and context adaptation in a unified end-to-end framework suitable for real-time control with strict latency guarantees.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a causally disentangled reinforcement learning framework that explicitly models latent confounders and non-stationary regime shifts for robust zero-shot generalization across diverse financial market environments, enabling policies to adapt instantaneously to unseen market shocks without fine-tuning.

BACKGROUND:  
Current zero-shot generalization methods in reinforcement learning struggle with abrupt, non-stationary regime shifts and latent confounding factors inherent in financial markets, causing significant performance degradation when deployed in unseen market conditions. Existing works either rely on context embedding without causal inference or focus on static environment assumptions, failing to capture evolving market dynamics and hidden causal drivers critical for robust decision-making under zero-shot conditions.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel framework combining causal disentanglement with a latent variable dynamic model that learns interpretable representations of market regimes, confounders, and shocks directly from market data. The method leverages a structured latent space informed by causal graphs inferred from historical financial datasets to isolate invariant causal mechanisms from spurious correlations. A policy network conditioned on these disentangled causal states is trained via offline reinforcement learning with pessimistic policy optimization to ensure robust zero-shot transfer. Additionally, an adaptive regime detection module triggers dynamic policy modulation without requiring environment interaction, enabling immediate adaptation to new market states.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on three benchmark financial market simulators/datasets: (1) **Fi-OpenSim** (a high-fidelity market microstructure simulator), (2) **UCI Forex Historical Data**, and (3) **Lobster Limit Order Book Dataset**. Baselines include **Pessimistic Proximal Policy Optimization (PPPO)**, **Inferring Behavior-Specific Context (IBC)**, and **Equilibrium Policy Generalization (EPG)**. Metrics include average cumulative return, Sharpe ratio, and zero-shot adaptation speed (measured by return recovery within first 1000 steps of unseen regime), targeting ≥5% absolute improvement in Sharpe ratio and ≥20% faster adaptation vs strongest baseline. Experiments run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds; ablation studies isolate contributions of causal disentanglement, latent confounder modeling, and regime detection.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works that either embed static context or optimize over pessimistic value estimates without causal structure, our approach explicitly disentangles latent confounders and models non-stationary market dynamics via causal latent variables, enabling true zero-shot adaptation to abrupt market regime shifts without requiring fine-tuning or additional environment data. This causal modeling of evolving financial regimes coupled with adaptive policy modulation is a first in zero-shot RL for financial markets.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA:  
We propose a Causal Invariant Representation Learning framework that explicitly disentangles and leverages stable causal mechanisms from high-dimensional sensory inputs under partial observability to enable zero-shot generalization of robotic policies in dynamic industrial environments with real-time safety constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Current reinforcement learning methods for robotics in dynamic industrial settings fail to maintain robust zero-shot generalization because they rely heavily on correlational context representations that shift with noisy sensor inputs, moving machinery, and changing human interactions. Existing approaches do not explicitly identify or exploit invariant causal structures under partial observability and safety-critical constraints, leading to brittle policies that degrade drastically when faced with unseen environment dynamics or sensor perturbations.

APPROACH:  
Our method integrates a structural causal model (SCM) inference module with a temporally-aware encoder that disentangles invariant causal factors from spurious correlations in sensor data streams. We then impose invariance regularization during policy learning to ensure the learned policy depends solely on stable causal variables robust across environment shifts. To handle partial observability and real-time safety, we incorporate a safety-constrained causal belief updater that maintains uncertainty-aware causal state estimates for safe decision-making. Policies are trained end-to-end with an augmented reinforcement learning objective combining causal invariance loss, safety constraint satisfaction, and reward maximization.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on the Meta-World industrial robotics suite, the AI2THOR robotic manipulation environment, and the OpenAI ProcGen benchmark adapted for dynamic industrial-like scenarios. Baselines include Inferring Behavior-Specific Context (2024), PERM (2025), and Equilibrium Policy Generalization (2025). Metrics include zero-shot success rate (>10% absolute improvement over Inferring Behavior-Specific Context), safety constraint violation rate (reduce by 50%), and cumulative episodic reward (improve by >5%) on unseen environment variants. Experiments run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, with 5 random seeds per setting, including ablations of the SCM module and safety belief updater.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior context-based or pessimistic optimization approaches, our framework explicitly models and enforces causal invariance under partial observability with real-time safety constraints, enabling robust zero-shot generalization in environments with dynamically shifting causal relations—a capability absent in current state-of-the-art methods.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hybrid quantum-classical continual learning framework that incrementally updates quantum kernel models with adaptive error mitigation to achieve real-time, low-latency streaming similarity computations while enabling provable robustness and continual adaptation to concept drift in dynamic data streams.

BACKGROUND:  
Current quantum kernel methods for streaming machine learning, including the Incremental Quantum Kernel Estimation approach, focus primarily on latency reduction by reusing partial kernel computations but do not address robustness to noise and concept drift inherent in real-world streaming data. Moreover, existing methods lack mechanisms for continual learning, which is critical for adapting models over time without full retraining. This results in degraded accuracy or stale models that fail to generalize to evolving data distributions under realistic noisy quantum hardware conditions.

APPROACH:  
We propose an incremental quantum kernel learning architecture that (1) integrates adaptive quantum error mitigation techniques tailored for streaming kernels to maintain fidelity under noise, (2) incorporates a continual learning protocol by leveraging memory-efficient quantum-classical hybrid updates that selectively refresh kernel components only when drift is detected, and (3) employs a dynamic confidence scoring system based on quantum state overlaps to trigger kernel recalibration dynamically. This design enables end-to-end streaming similarity computation with kernel update latency below 15 ms while continuously adapting to data distribution changes without full kernel recomputation.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on Real-time Human Activity Recognition (HAR), Google Speech Commands V2, and UCI Gas Sensor Array Drift datasets to benchmark adaptation under concept drift and noise. Baselines include Incremental Quantum Kernel Estimation (IQKE), classical streaming kernel SVM with Nyström approximation, and full quantum kernel recomputation. Metrics: kernel update latency (<15 ms target), classification accuracy (aiming for ≤1% drop vs full recomputation), and drift adaptation measured by accuracy recovery after distribution shifts (>5% improvement vs IQKE without adaptation). Experiments run on IBM Quantum hardware simulators with noise modeling and real quantum processors, with 5 random seeds and ablation on error mitigation and drift detection modules.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work which only incrementally updates kernel estimates for latency, our method uniquely combines incremental updates with adaptive error mitigation and continual learning mechanisms to robustly handle concept drift in noisy quantum streaming scenarios, a capability not addressed by existing quantum kernel estimation methods or classical streaming kernel approaches.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hierarchical, memory- and compute-efficient quantum kernel embedding framework that combines incremental kernel matrix construction with adaptive quantum feature pruning and classical-quantum hybrid compression to enable scalable, high-accuracy text classification on large, high-dimensional NLP datasets.

BACKGROUND:  
Quantum kernel methods for text classification face a critical scalability bottleneck due to the quadratic memory requirement of storing large kernel Gram matrices, especially for high-dimensional, sparse NLP embeddings. Existing approaches like memory-efficient quantum kernel embedding reduce memory usage but are limited to incremental kernel estimation without adaptive feature selection or hybrid compression, restricting their applicability to truly large-scale datasets and limiting accuracy. The open problem is how to enable quantum kernel methods to scale to real-world, large-scale NLP tasks while maintaining or improving classification accuracy and resource efficiency beyond established baselines.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical quantum kernel embedding approach where (1) the kernel Gram matrix is constructed incrementally through mini-batch quantum kernel estimations that are progressively merged, (2) an adaptive quantum feature pruning mechanism dynamically identifies and removes low-importance quantum circuit features guided by classical sparsity metrics and quantum feature importance scores, and (3) a hybrid classical-quantum low-rank compression step compresses intermediate kernel representations using quantum-inspired classical algorithms before full kernel assembly. This pipeline drastically reduces peak memory usage beyond 50%, maintains kernel expressivity, and improves scalability. The method is validated end-to-end on large-scale NLP embeddings encoded with state-of-the-art transformers.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate the approach on the 20 Newsgroups, AG News, and Reuters-21578 datasets, comparing against baselines including full quantum kernel Gram matrix embedding, the original memory-efficient quantum kernel embedding, and classical classical kernel SVMs (RBF kernel) and BERT-base embeddings combined with classical classifiers. Metrics include classification accuracy (target: within 3% of full kernel), peak memory usage reduction (>60% reduction), and training latency (target: <30% overhead vs classical SVM). Experiments run on quantum simulators with noise models reflecting NISQ hardware, using 5 random seeds and ablation studies for pruning thresholds and compression ranks.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that focuses solely on incremental kernel estimation for memory efficiency, this method introduces adaptive quantum feature pruning and hybrid classical-quantum compression, enabling scalable kernel embedding on larger NLP datasets with higher dimensionality and sparsity while preserving or improving accuracy. This is the first approach to jointly optimize kernel construction and representation compression in a memory-aware quantum kernel embedding framework for large-scale text classification.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scheduling-aware adaptive variational quantum circuit framework that dynamically optimizes quantum machine learning models by jointly leveraging noise variability prediction and quantum resource contention data to achieve provable quantum advantage in noisy multi-tenant cloud environments on real-world large-scale ML benchmarks.

BACKGROUND:  
Current variational quantum circuit optimization methods assume static noise models and exclusive hardware access, which fails to capture the fluctuating noise and contention effects prevalent in multi-tenant cloud quantum computing platforms. This gap leads to unstable performance and loss of quantum advantage in practical machine learning tasks. There is a critical need to incorporate real-time multi-tenant scheduling and noise variability data into adaptive quantum circuit training to maintain fidelity and performance in cloud-hosted quantum ML services.

APPROACH:  
We propose a robust multi-tenant scheduling-aware adaptive variational circuit optimization framework that integrates (1) predictive noise modeling derived from multi-tenant quantum cloud trace datasets, (2) real-time scheduling and hardware contention signals, and (3) an adaptive circuit parameter update policy guided by a multi-objective loss that balances classification accuracy and circuit fidelity. The method dynamically adjusts quantum circuit depth, gate sequences, and parameter gradients to mitigate noise-induced errors proactively during training and inference. This framework also incorporates a quantum-aware regularization scheme to preserve expressivity under fluctuating noise conditions.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the quantum versions of the MNIST dataset, Fashion-MNIST, and the QMLBench quantum chemistry dataset, benchmarking against fixed-noise optimization methods such as standard VQE, QAOA with Adam optimizer, and Robust VQC scheduling-agnostic baselines. Metrics include classification accuracy (target >90% stable accuracy), fidelity retention (>95% fidelity under noise fluctuations), and runtime overhead (<15% increase compared to baseline optimizers). Experiments will run on IBM Quantum cloud hardware and Qiskit simulator noise models with multi-tenant trace replay. Protocol includes 5 random seeds per setup, ablation of scheduling-awareness and noise prediction components, and resource overhead analysis under varying contention levels.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works that assume static noise or exclusive quantum hardware access (e.g., standard variational circuit optimization and quantum advantage demonstrations focused on idealized settings), this approach uniquely incorporates real-time multi-tenant scheduling and dynamic noise modeling into the quantum circuit adaptation loop, demonstrating provable robustness and sustained quantum advantage on practical ML tasks under realistic cloud quantum computing conditions.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a privacy-preserving quantum feature encoding framework that leverages adaptive quantum circuit architectures with provable differential privacy guarantees and functional representability control to enable secure, high-accuracy healthcare classification on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices.

BACKGROUND:  
Current quantum machine learning pipelines for healthcare classification fail to simultaneously guarantee patient data privacy and maintain strong predictive performance, as encoded quantum states leak sensitive information during intermediate processing or measurement. Existing privacy-preserving quantum encodings either lack formal differential privacy guarantees or are incompatible with expressive quantum models needed for complex healthcare data. This work addresses the open problem of achieving rigorous quantum differential privacy while preserving model expressivity and accuracy in practical NISQ-era quantum classifiers.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel quantum encoding scheme combining adaptive parameterized quantum circuits structured to limit representability of sensitive features with built-in noise injection calibrated for quantum differential privacy. This method integrates controlled non-Clifford gates and randomized quantum feature maps that obfuscate patient-identifiable information yet retain the function class necessary for effective classification. Privacy guarantees are mathematically derived from the encoding noise and circuit design, ensuring resistance to quantum state tomography and inference attacks. The encoded data is processed by hybrid quantum-classical variational classifiers optimized under resource constraints typical of NISQ platforms.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark healthcare datasets: MIMIC-III (ICU patient data), Breast Cancer Wisconsin (Diagnostic), and the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI). Compare against baselines: Privacy-Preserving Quantum Data Encoding for Healthcare Classification (the tournament winner method), Quantum Kernel Estimation (QKE) classifiers, and classical Differentially Private Random Forests (DP-RF). Quantitatively measure classification accuracy, quantum differential privacy epsilon values (targeting ε < 1), and robustness to quantum state tomography attacks, aiming for ≥3% absolute accuracy improvement over prior privacy-preserving quantum methods, while maintaining privacy budgets comparable to classical approaches. Conduct experiments on IBM Quantum hardware with 5-15 qubits, running 5 seeds per setup, including ablations on noise levels and circuit depth.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that either compromises privacy or model accuracy, this approach achieves rigorous quantum differential privacy with explicit function representability control enabling expressive yet secure quantum models. It addresses the gap in existing frameworks by jointly optimizing privacy-by-design encoding with adaptive circuit architectures tailored to NISQ constraints, providing provable privacy guarantees and empirically demonstrated advantage in healthcare classification tasks.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a noise-adaptive variational quantum circuit (VQC) framework that dynamically optimizes circuit expressivity and depth during training to maximize quantum advantage in supervised learning tasks on NISQ hardware.

BACKGROUND:  
Current variational quantum circuits for NISQ machine learning suffer from a fundamental tradeoff between expressivity and noise resilience: deeper circuits enable richer function representations but are increasingly vulnerable to decoherence and gate errors, hindering reliable feature learning. Existing approaches employ fixed ansatzes and static noise mitigation, failing to optimize this tradeoff dynamically, which limits their performance and practical quantum advantage on real noisy devices.

APPROACH:  
We propose a noise-adaptive VQC training protocol that integrates real-time quantum hardware noise profiling and a trainable expressivity controller module to automatically adjust circuit depth, gate selection (including non-Clifford gates), and parameterization complexity during optimization. This controller uses reinforcement learning to balance expressivity against noise impact, guided by hardware noise metrics and model training loss, enabling an optimal expressivity-noise tradeoff. We embed noise-aware regularization directly into the loss function and leverage hardware-efficient ansatzes with modular subcircuits that can be selectively activated or pruned on-the-fly to maintain model robustness and generalization.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted on three benchmark supervised learning datasets: MNIST (handwritten digit classification), Fashion-MNIST (clothing classification), and the UCI Wine Quality dataset (regression). Baselines include fixed-ansatz VQCs such as the Hardware-Efficient Ansatz (HEA), the Quantum Alternating Operator Ansatz (QAOA-based), and classical baselines like Random Forest and multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) with AdamW optimizer. Metrics will report classification accuracy improvements exceeding +3% absolute over fixed VQC baselines on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, regression RMSE reduction by >5% on Wine Quality, and stability measured by variance across 10 runs with different noise profiles and random seeds. Experiments will be run on IBM Quantum hardware (e.g., ibmq_mumbai, ibmq_casablanca) with a training compute budget of 48 hours per model, including ablation studies on controller components, noise regularization terms, and ansatz modularity.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work which statically designs VQC ansatzes and relies on post hoc noise mitigation, our method actively and adaptively controls circuit expressivity in direct response to real-time noise characterization and training feedback, enabling reliable feature learning on NISQ devices without manual tuning. This dynamic expressivity-noise tradeoff optimization is a capability absent from frameworks like those in "Prospects for quantum advantage in machine learning from the representability of functions" or noise-resilient circuits that do not incorporate reinforcement learning-based adaptive control.
